This workflow covers installing ly, switching from a graphical display manager, enabling the correct ly systemd service, selecting the i3 session, fixing remembered Plasma sessions through dmrc and AccountsService, and switching back if needed.

Section: Set dmrc session to i3

Set the user's dmrc session to i3. This helps display managers remember the intended X11 session.

Code (bash):
nano ~/.dmrc

Code (bash):
[Desktop]
Session=i3

Code (bash):
cat ~/.dmrc

title: i3 Login with ly on Arch
description: Configure ly as a terminal-style display manager on Arch and make it start the i3 session by default.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: i3 Login with ly on Arch

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Configure ly as a terminal-style display manager on Arch and make it start the i3 session by default.

Intro: Configure ly as a terminal-style login manager for an i3 setup on Arch. This workflow covers installing ly, switching from a graphical display manager, enabling the correct ly systemd service, selecting the i3 session, fixing remembered Plasma sessions through dmrc and AccountsService, and switching back if needed.

Section: Fix AccountsService remembered session

If the system keeps opening Plasma even after selecting i3, check the per-user AccountsService session. In this setup, AccountsService remembered Plasma and had to be changed to i3.

Code (bash):
sudo cat /var/lib/AccountsService/users/$USER

If the file contains XSession=plasma, edit it and change the remembered X session to i3. Replace $USER with the target username on another machine.

Code (bash):
sudo nano /var/lib/AccountsService/users/$USER

Code (bash):
[User]
Session=
XSession=i3
Icon=/home/$USER/.face
SystemAccount=false

- The important value is XSession=i3.
- If XSession=plasma is present, the login manager can keep opening Plasma.
- After changing the file, reboot and choose i3 in ly once.

Title: i3 Login with ly on Arch

Description: Configure ly as a terminal-style display manager on Arch and make it start the i3 session by default.

Intro: Configure ly as a terminal-style login manager for an i3 setup on Arch. This workflow covers installing ly, switching from a graphical display manager, enabling the correct ly systemd service, selecting the i3 session, fixing remembered Plasma sessions through dmrc and AccountsService, and switching back if needed.

Section: Verify active display manager and session

Use these commands after logging in to confirm that ly is active and the i3 session files are available.

Code (bash):
systemctl status display-manager --no-pager
systemctl status ly@tty2.service --no-pager
ls /usr/share/xsessions
cat ~/.dmrc
sudo cat /var/lib/AccountsService/users/$USER

Section: Switch back to LightDM if needed

If ly does not work as expected or a graphical login screen is preferred again, disable ly and restore LightDM.

Code (bash):
sudo systemctl disable ly@tty2.service
sudo systemctl enable getty@tty2.service

title: i3 PipeWire Sound Checks on Arch
description: Check and fix common PipeWire sound issues in a minimal i3 session on Arch.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: i3 PipeWire Sound Checks on Arch

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Check and fix common PipeWire sound issues in a minimal i3 session on Arch.

Intro: Check and fix sound in a minimal i3 session on Arch using PipeWire, WirePlumber, pipewire-pulse, and wpctl. This workflow focuses on the common case where sound appears broken because the default sink volume is muted or set to 0.00.

Section: Check PipeWire status

On a fresh Arch install with PipeWire and WirePlumber running, sound can still appear broken when the default sink volume is simply at 0.00. Check status first before assuming anything is actually wrong.

Code (bash):
wpctl status
wpctl get-volume @DEFAULT_AUDIO_SINK@

title: i3 PipeWire Sound Checks on Arch
description: Check and fix common PipeWire sound issues in a minimal i3 session on Arch.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: i3 PipeWire Sound Checks on Arch

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Check and fix common PipeWire sound issues in a minimal i3 session on Arch.

Intro: Check and fix sound in a minimal i3 session on Arch using PipeWire, WirePlumber, pipewire-pulse, and wpctl. This workflow focuses on the common case where sound appears broken because the default sink volume is muted or set to 0.00.

Section: Set default output volume

Set, raise, lower, or mute the default output device with wpctl.

Code (bash):
wpctl set-volume @DEFAULT_AUDIO_SINK@ 0.5
wpctl set-volume @DEFAULT_AUDIO_SINK@ 5%+
wpctl set-volume @DEFAULT_AUDIO_SINK@ 5%-
wpctl set-mute @DEFAULT_AUDIO_SINK@ toggle

- 0.5 means 50 percent volume.
- 5%+ raises volume by 5 percent.
- 5%- lowers volume by 5 percent.
- set-mute toggle toggles mute on the default output sink.

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Check and fix common PipeWire sound issues in a minimal i3 session on Arch.

Intro: Check and fix sound in a minimal i3 session on Arch using PipeWire, WirePlumber, pipewire-pulse, and wpctl. This workflow focuses on the common case where sound appears broken because the default sink volume is muted or set to 0.00.

Section: Debug commands

Code (bash):
# Check audio graph and default devices
wpctl status

# Check default sink volume
wpctl get-volume @DEFAULT_AUDIO_SINK@

# Check user services
systemctl --user status pipewire
systemctl --user status wireplumber
systemctl --user status pipewire-pulse

# Check recent user service logs
journalctl --user -u pipewire -b --no-pager -n 100
journalctl --user -u wireplumber -b --no-pager -n 100
journalctl --user -u pipewire-pulse -b --no-pager -n 100

description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: i3 Screen Layout Profiles with xrandr Refresh Rates

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.

Intro: Create reusable screen layout profiles for an i3 setup on Arch. This workflow keeps display switching separate from the main i3 setup by using xrandr scripts for laptop-only, external 1K, external 2K, and mirror modes. Each profile sets the expected refresh rate and can be checked with xrandr --current after switching.

Section: Install required tools

Install xrandr for display detection and layout changes. Install xsetroot only if the main i3 setup has not already installed it for root background color.

Code (bash):
sudo pacman -S xorg-xrandr xorg-xsetroot

title: i3 Screen Layout Profiles with xrandr Refresh Rates
description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: i3 Screen Layout Profiles with xrandr Refresh Rates

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.

Intro: Create reusable screen layout profiles for an i3 setup on Arch. This workflow keeps display switching separate from the main i3 setup by using xrandr scripts for laptop-only, external 1K, external 2K, and mirror modes. Each profile sets the expected refresh rate and can be checked with xrandr --current after switching.

Section: Identify display outputs

Run xrandr from the current i3 session and note the connected output names. These names are used directly in the screen layout scripts.

Code (bash):
xrandr | grep " connected"
xrandr

On this setup, the laptop panel appears as DP-2 when only the laptop screen is active. External outputs can appear as HDMI-0, DP-0, DP-1, or another driver-specific name depending on the cable, dock, GPU path, and monitor.

Code (bash):
# Example laptop-only result
DP-2 connected primary 1920x1080+0+0

- connected means the output is detected by xrandr.
- primary means the output is the main screen for panels, bars, and workspace placement.
- The laptop panel name is not universal. On this machine it is DP-2, but other laptops may use eDP-1, eDP-1-1, or LVDS-1.
- Always confirm the external output name after connecting a new monitor or projector.

Source type: Doc

Title: i3 Screen Layout Profiles with xrandr Refresh Rates

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.

Intro: Create reusable screen layout profiles for an i3 setup on Arch. This workflow keeps display switching separate from the main i3 setup by using xrandr scripts for laptop-only, external 1K, external 2K, and mirror modes. Each profile sets the expected refresh rate and can be checked with xrandr --current after switching.

Section: Create the laptop-only profile

The laptop-only profile is the safe default and recovery mode. It makes the DP-2 laptop panel primary at 1920x1080 and 144 Hz, then turns off known external outputs.

Code (bash):
nano ~/.screenlayout/laptop-only.sh

Code (bash):
#!/bin/bash

xrandr \
 --output DP-2 --primary --mode 1920x1080 --rate 144 \
 --output HDMI-0 --off \
 --output DP-0 --off \
 --output DP-1 --off

title: i3 Screen Layout Profiles with xrandr Refresh Rates
description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: i3 Screen Layout Profiles with xrandr Refresh Rates

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.

Intro: Create reusable screen layout profiles for an i3 setup on Arch. This workflow keeps display switching separate from the main i3 setup by using xrandr scripts for laptop-only, external 1K, external 2K, and mirror modes. Each profile sets the expected refresh rate and can be checked with xrandr --current after switching.

Section: Create the external 1K profile

The external 1K profile is for normal 1920x1080 external monitors on HDMI-0 at 60 Hz. If HDMI-0 is not connected, it falls back to laptop-only.

Code (bash):
nano ~/.screenlayout/external-1k-only.sh

Code (bash):
#!/bin/bash

if xrandr | grep -q "^HDMI-0 connected"
then
 xrandr \
 --output HDMI-0 --primary --mode 1920x1080 --rate 60 \
 --output DP-2 --off
else
 ~/.screenlayout/laptop-only.sh
fi

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.

Intro: Create reusable screen layout profiles for an i3 setup on Arch. This workflow keeps display switching separate from the main i3 setup by using xrandr scripts for laptop-only, external 1K, external 2K, and mirror modes. Each profile sets the expected refresh rate and can be checked with xrandr --current after switching.

Section: Create the external 2K profile

The external 2K profile is for the office desk monitor on HDMI-0 at 2560x1440 and 74.93 Hz. If HDMI-0 is not connected, it falls back to laptop-only.

Code (bash):
nano ~/.screenlayout/external-2k-only.sh

Code (bash):
#!/bin/bash

if xrandr | grep -q "^HDMI-0 connected"
then
 xrandr \
 --output HDMI-0 --primary --mode 2560x1440 --rate 74.93 \
 --output DP-2 --off
else
 ~/.screenlayout/laptop-only.sh
fi

Source type: Doc

Title: i3 Screen Layout Profiles with xrandr Refresh Rates

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.

Intro: Create reusable screen layout profiles for an i3 setup on Arch. This workflow keeps display switching separate from the main i3 setup by using xrandr scripts for laptop-only, external 1K, external 2K, and mirror modes. Each profile sets the expected refresh rate and can be checked with xrandr --current after switching.

Section: Create the mirror profile

The mirror profile is for presentations. Mirroring uses 1920x1080 at 60 Hz on both screens because projectors, TVs, meeting-room screens, and capture devices are more likely to support it.

Code (bash):
nano ~/.screenlayout/mirror-1k.sh

Code (bash):
#!/bin/bash

if xrandr | grep -q "^HDMI-0 connected"
then
 xrandr \
 --output DP-2 --primary --mode 1920x1080 --rate 60 \
 --output HDMI-0 --mode 1920x1080 --rate 60 --same-as DP-2
else
 ~/.screenlayout/laptop-only.sh
fi

text:
Source type: Doc

Title: i3 Screen Layout Profiles with xrandr Refresh Rates

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.

Intro: Create reusable screen layout profiles for an i3 setup on Arch. This workflow keeps display switching separate from the main i3 setup by using xrandr scripts for laptop-only, external 1K, external 2K, and mirror modes. Each profile sets the expected refresh rate and can be checked with xrandr --current after switching.

Section: Make profiles executable and test

Make all profile scripts executable. Run a profile, then use xrandr --current to verify the active mode and refresh rate. The active refresh rate is marked with *.

Code (bash):
chmod +x ~/.screenlayout/laptop-only.sh
chmod +x ~/.screenlayout/external-1k-only.sh
chmod +x ~/.screenlayout/external-2k-only.sh
chmod +x ~/.screenlayout/mirror-1k.sh

~/.screenlayout/laptop-only.sh
xrandr --current

~/.screenlayout/external-1k-only.sh
xrandr --current

~/.screenlayout/external-2k-only.sh
xrandr --current

~/.screenlayout/mirror-1k.sh
xrandr --current

- If the external monitor is not connected, the external scripts fall back to laptop-only.
- If the external monitor appears under a different output name, replace HDMI-0 in the scripts with the detected output.
- Keep laptop-only as the default because it is the safest recovery mode after hibernate or monitor disconnects.

Title: i3 Screen Layout Profiles with xrandr Refresh Rates

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.

Intro: Create reusable screen layout profiles for an i3 setup on Arch. This workflow keeps display switching separate from the main i3 setup by using xrandr scripts for laptop-only, external 1K, external 2K, and mirror modes. Each profile sets the expected refresh rate and can be checked with xrandr --current after switching.

Section: Check active refresh rates

Use focused checks when confirming a known profile. These commands show the connected output and the mode line where xrandr marks the active refresh rate.

Code (bash):
# Laptop-only expected active marker:
# 1920x1080 60.00 + 144.00*
~/.screenlayout/laptop-only.sh
xrandr --current | grep -A1 "DP-2 connected"

# External 2K expected active marker:
# 2560x1440 59.95 + 74.93*
~/.screenlayout/external-2k-only.sh
xrandr --current | grep -A1 "HDMI-0 connected"

# External 1K expected active marker:
# 1920x1080 60.00*
~/.screenlayout/external-1k-only.sh
xrandr --current | grep -A1 "HDMI-0 connected"

# Mirror mode check
~/.screenlayout/mirror-1k.sh
xrandr --current | grep -E "DP-2 connected|HDMI-0 connected|1920x1080"

sectionTitle: Add i3 startup and screen shortcuts
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: i3 Screen Layout Profiles with xrandr Refresh Rates

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.

Intro: Create reusable screen layout profiles for an i3 setup on Arch. This workflow keeps display switching separate from the main i3 setup by using xrandr scripts for laptop-only, external 1K, external 2K, and mirror modes. Each profile sets the expected refresh rate and can be checked with xrandr --current after switching.

Section: Add i3 startup and screen shortcuts

Add the laptop-only startup profile and screen profile shortcuts to ~/.config/i3/config. This keeps i3 startup safe while allowing manual switching to external-only or mirror mode.

Code (bash):
nano ~/.config/i3/config

Code (bash):
# Safe default display profile
exec --no-startup-id ~/.screenlayout/laptop-only.sh

# Recommended single-screen workspace mapping
workspace 1 output primary
workspace 2 output primary
workspace 3 output primary
workspace 4 output primary
workspace 5 output primary
workspace 6 output primary

# Screen profile shortcuts
bindsym $mod+Control+1 exec --no-startup-id ~/.screenlayout/laptop-only.sh
bindsym $mod+Control+2 exec --no-startup-id ~/.screenlayout/external-1k-only.sh
bindsym $mod+Control+3 exec --no-startup-id ~/.screenlayout/external-2k-only.sh
bindsym $mod+Control+4 exec --no-startup-id ~/.screenlayout/mirror-1k.sh

- No shortcut change is required if these bindings already exist.
- Mod+Control+1 forces laptop-only mode and is useful as a recovery shortcut after a bad resume or disconnected monitor.
- Mod+Control+2 switches to a 1920x1080 external-only profile at 60 Hz.
- Mod+Control+3 switches to a 2560x1440 external-only profile at 74.93 Hz.
- Mod+Control+4 switches to 1920x1080 mirror mode at 60 Hz for presentations.
- Avoid Mod+Shift+J, Mod+Shift+K, and Mod+Shift+L for screen modes because default i3 uses them for moving windows.

Section: Reload i3 and verify keybindings

Reload i3 after editing the config. If i3 reports duplicate keybindings, search for the conflicting bindsym line and keep only one binding for that key combination.

Code (bash):

grep -nE 'screenlayout|Control\+[1234]' ~/.config/i3/config

Title: i3 Screen Layout Profiles with xrandr Refresh Rates

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Create reusable xrandr screen layout profiles with explicit refresh rates for laptop-only, external-only, and mirror modes in an i3 setup.

Intro: Create reusable screen layout profiles for an i3 setup on Arch. This workflow keeps display switching separate from the main i3 setup by using xrandr scripts for laptop-only, external 1K, external 2K, and mirror modes. Each profile sets the expected refresh rate and can be checked with xrandr --current after switching.

Section: Debug commands

Code (bash):
# Monitor names, positions, modes, and refresh rates
xrandr --current
xrandr --current | grep " connected"

# Confirm screen layout scripts exist
ls -l ~/.screenlayout

# Test the safe laptop profile
~/.screenlayout/laptop-only.sh
xrandr --current | grep -A1 "DP-2 connected"

# Test an external profile after connecting the monitor
~/.screenlayout/external-1k-only.sh
xrandr --current | grep -A1 "HDMI-0 connected"

~/.screenlayout/external-2k-only.sh
xrandr --current | grep -A1 "HDMI-0 connected"

# Test mirror mode after connecting the monitor
~/.screenlayout/mirror-1k.sh
xrandr --current | grep -E "DP-2 connected|HDMI-0 connected|1920x1080"

# Check screen profile keybindings
grep -nE 'screenlayout|Control\+[1234]' ~/.config/i3/config

# Find duplicate keybindings manually if i3 reports a conflict
grep -n '^bindsym' ~/.config/i3/config

Title: i3 Screenshot Workflow with maim and xclip

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Configure a simple selected-region screenshot shortcut in i3 using maim, slop, and xclip.

Intro: Configure screenshots in i3 with maim and xclip. This workflow captures a selected region and copies the image directly to the clipboard, which keeps screenshot handling fast and simple inside a minimal i3 setup.

Section: Add the i3 screenshot shortcut

Add a keybinding to ~/.config/i3/config. The F8 shortcut captures a selected region and copies it to the clipboard as PNG data.

Code (bash):
nano ~/.config/i3/config

Code (bash):
# Screenshots
bindsym F8 exec maim -s | xclip -selection clipboard -t image/png

Title: i3 Touchpad Scrolling on Arch

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Configure natural scrolling, tap-to-click, and slower touchpad scrolling on Arch with i3, X11, and libinput.

Intro: Configure touchpad behavior on Arch in an i3/X11 session with libinput. This setup enables natural scrolling, enables tap-to-click, slows two-finger scrolling with ScrollPixelDistance, and persists the settings across reboot and future i3 logins.

Section: Install the X11 input tools

Install the libinput Xorg driver and xinput command-line tool if they are not already present.

Code (bash):
sudo pacman -S xf86-input-libinput xorg-xinput

Title: i3 Touchpad Scrolling on Arch

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Configure natural scrolling, tap-to-click, and slower touchpad scrolling on Arch with i3, X11, and libinput.

Intro: Configure touchpad behavior on Arch in an i3/X11 session with libinput. This setup enables natural scrolling, enables tap-to-click, slows two-finger scrolling with ScrollPixelDistance, and persists the settings across reboot and future i3 logins.

Section: Find the touchpad name

List input devices and copy the full touchpad device name. Do not rely on the numeric device id because it can change after reboot.

Code (bash):
xinput list

# Example touchpad name from this ASUS setup:
# ASUF1204:00 2808:0202 Touchpad

title: i3 Touchpad Scrolling on Arch
description: Configure natural scrolling, tap-to-click, and slower touchpad scrolling on Arch with i3, X11, and libinput.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: i3 Touchpad Scrolling on Arch

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Configure natural scrolling, tap-to-click, and slower touchpad scrolling on Arch with i3, X11, and libinput.

Intro: Configure touchpad behavior on Arch in an i3/X11 session with libinput. This setup enables natural scrolling, enables tap-to-click, slows two-finger scrolling with ScrollPixelDistance, and persists the settings across reboot and future i3 logins.

Section: Inspect current properties

Inspect the current libinput properties for the touchpad. The exact property ids can change, so use the property names in commands.

Code (bash):
xinput list-props "ASUF1204:00 2808:0202 Touchpad"

Table:
Property | Useful value
libinput Natural Scrolling Enabled | 0 is traditional scrolling, 1 is natural scrolling
libinput Tapping Enabled | 0 disables tap-to-click, 1 enables tap-to-click
libinput Scroll Method Enabled | 1, 0, 0 means two-finger scrolling is active
libinput Scrolling Pixel Distance | Higher values feel slower, lower values feel faster

title: i3 Touchpad Scrolling on Arch
description: Configure natural scrolling, tap-to-click, and slower touchpad scrolling on Arch with i3, X11, and libinput.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: i3 Touchpad Scrolling on Arch

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Configure natural scrolling, tap-to-click, and slower touchpad scrolling on Arch with i3, X11, and libinput.

Intro: Configure touchpad behavior on Arch in an i3/X11 session with libinput. This setup enables natural scrolling, enables tap-to-click, slows two-finger scrolling with ScrollPixelDistance, and persists the settings across reboot and future i3 logins.

Section: Live-test the settings

Set the properties live first. These commands affect the current X11 session only and are useful for choosing a comfortable scroll distance before making the setup permanent.

Code (bash):
xinput set-prop "ASUF1204:00 2808:0202 Touchpad" "libinput Natural Scrolling Enabled" 1
xinput set-prop "ASUF1204:00 2808:0202 Touchpad" "libinput Tapping Enabled" 1
xinput set-prop "ASUF1204:00 2808:0202 Touchpad" "libinput Scrolling Pixel Distance" 40

- Natural scrolling reverses the touchpad scroll direction to the mac-style direction.
- Tapping enables tap-to-click.
- A ScrollPixelDistance value of 40 worked well on this setup.
- Increase ScrollPixelDistance for slower scrolling.
- Decrease ScrollPixelDistance for faster scrolling.

xinput list-props "ASUF1204:00 2808:0202 Touchpad" | grep -E "Natural Scrolling|Tapping Enabled|Scrolling Pixel Distance"

title: i3 Touchpad Scrolling on Arch
description: Configure natural scrolling, tap-to-click, and slower touchpad scrolling on Arch with i3, X11, and libinput.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: i3 Touchpad Scrolling on Arch

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Configure natural scrolling, tap-to-click, and slower touchpad scrolling on Arch with i3, X11, and libinput.

Intro: Configure touchpad behavior on Arch in an i3/X11 session with libinput. This setup enables natural scrolling, enables tap-to-click, slows two-finger scrolling with ScrollPixelDistance, and persists the settings across reboot and future i3 logins.

Section: Make the setup permanent

Persist the touchpad settings with an Xorg input class. This is an Xorg/libinput configuration, not an i3 configuration.

Code (bash):
sudo mkdir -p /etc/X11/xorg.conf.d
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf.d/30-touchpad.conf

Code (bash):
Section "InputClass"
 Identifier "ASUS touchpad libinput"
 MatchIsTouchpad "on"
 Driver "libinput"

 Option "NaturalScrolling" "true"
 Option "Tapping" "true"
 Option "ScrollMethod" "twofinger"
 Option "DisableWhileTyping" "true"
 Option "ScrollPixelDistance" "40"
EndSection

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Configure natural scrolling, tap-to-click, and slower touchpad scrolling on Arch with i3, X11, and libinput.

Intro: Configure touchpad behavior on Arch in an i3/X11 session with libinput. This setup enables natural scrolling, enables tap-to-click, slows two-finger scrolling with ScrollPixelDistance, and persists the settings across reboot and future i3 logins.

Section: Restart the X11 session

Restart the i3/X11 session after changing the Xorg input file. Reloading i3 is not enough because Xorg reads this input configuration when the graphical session starts.

Code (bash):
i3-msg exit

Log back into i3, then verify the permanent values.

Code (bash):
xinput list-props "ASUF1204:00 2808:0202 Touchpad" | grep -E "Natural Scrolling|Tapping Enabled|Scrolling Pixel Distance"

title: i3 Touchpad Scrolling on Arch
description: Configure natural scrolling, tap-to-click, and slower touchpad scrolling on Arch with i3, X11, and libinput.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: i3 Touchpad Scrolling on Arch

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Configure natural scrolling, tap-to-click, and slower touchpad scrolling on Arch with i3, X11, and libinput.

Intro: Configure touchpad behavior on Arch in an i3/X11 session with libinput. This setup enables natural scrolling, enables tap-to-click, slows two-finger scrolling with ScrollPixelDistance, and persists the settings across reboot and future i3 logins.

Section: Optional app-level tuning

Browser and terminal scroll feel can still be tuned separately. Picom does not control touchpad scrolling or smoothness, and true global smooth or inertia scrolling is not normally handled by X11/libinput.

For Alacritty, edit the terminal scrolling multiplier if terminal scrolling feels too fast or jumpy.

Code (bash):
nano ~/.config/alacritty/alacritty.toml

Code (toml):
[scrolling]
history = 10000
multiplier = 2

- Use multiplier = 1 for slower terminal scrolling.
- Use multiplier = 2 or 3 for faster terminal scrolling.
- In Firefox, check general.smoothScroll and apz.gtk.kinetic_scroll.enabled in about:config.
- Firefox settings are app-level browser settings, not system-wide touchpad settings.

title: Arch ASUS Laptop Controls
description: Use asusctl and asusd for ASUS-specific laptop controls such as keyboard RGB and battery charge limits.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch ASUS Laptop Controls

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Use asusctl and asusd for ASUS-specific laptop controls such as keyboard RGB and battery charge limits.

Intro: Configure ASUS-specific laptop controls on Arch with asusctl and asusd. This workflow covers power profile separation, ASUS keyboard RGB, battery charge limits, a local asusd service workaround, and set-and-go usage when ASUS settings persist without keeping the daemon enabled all day.

Section: Understand the tool split

ASUS laptops can expose extra hardware controls that are not part of standard i3, PipeWire, or powerprofilesctl workflows. Use asusctl only for ASUS-specific features such as keyboard RGB, battery charge limits, and optional fan curve inspection.

- Use powerprofilesctl for normal performance, balanced, and power-saver modes.
- Use asusctl for ASUS-specific laptop controls.
- The asusd service is required while running asusctl commands.
- If the settings persist after being applied, asusd does not have to run all day for a set-and-go setup.

Section: Check standard Linux power profiles

Use powerprofilesctl for normal Linux power mode changes. This is separate from ASUS-specific hardware control.

Code (bash):
powerprofilesctl
powerprofilesctl get

powerprofilesctl set balanced
powerprofilesctl set power-saver
powerprofilesctl set performance

title: Arch ASUS Laptop Controls
description: Use asusctl and asusd for ASUS-specific laptop controls such as keyboard RGB and battery charge limits.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch ASUS Laptop Controls

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Use asusctl and asusd for ASUS-specific laptop controls such as keyboard RGB and battery charge limits.

Intro: Configure ASUS-specific laptop controls on Arch with asusctl and asusd. This workflow covers power profile separation, ASUS keyboard RGB, battery charge limits, a local asusd service workaround, and set-and-go usage when ASUS settings persist without keeping the daemon enabled all day.

Section: ASUS service workaround

On this ASUS setup, the packaged asusd.service failed before the daemon started. The failure appeared as status 226/NAMESPACE on ExecStartPre=/bin/sleep 1. This points to systemd sandbox or namespace settings in the packaged unit, not an ASUS hardware failure.

The working fix was to create a simpler local systemd unit at /etc/systemd/system/asusd.service. A local unit in /etc overrides the packaged unit in /usr/lib/systemd/system.

Code (bash):
# Inspect the active service definition
systemctl cat asusd.service

# Check service status
systemctl status asusd.service --no-pager

# Check logs if it fails
journalctl -u asusd.service -b --no-pager -n 100

Code (bash):
# Create a minimal local asusd service
sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/asusd.service >/dev/null <<'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=ASUS Notebook Control
After=systemd-udevd.service

[Service]
Environment=IS_SERVICE=1
ExecStart=/usr/bin/asusd
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=1
Type=dbus
BusName=xyz.ljones.Asusd
TimeoutSec=10

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Remove old drop-in overrides if they were created during testing
sudo rm -rf /etc/systemd/system/asusd.service.d

# Reload systemd and start the service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl reset-failed asusd.service
sudo systemctl start asusd.service
systemctl status asusd.service --no-pager

Intro: Configure ASUS-specific laptop controls on Arch with asusctl and asusd. This workflow covers power profile separation, ASUS keyboard RGB, battery charge limits, a local asusd service workaround, and set-and-go usage when ASUS settings persist without keeping the daemon enabled all day.

Section: Enable asusd only if needed

Enable the local service only if ASUS controls should be available after reboot without starting the daemon manually.

Code (bash):
# Enable ASUS control daemon on boot
sudo systemctl enable --now asusd.service

# Verify boot state
systemctl is-enabled asusd.service
systemctl status asusd.service --no-pager

text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch ASUS Laptop Controls

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Use asusctl and asusd for ASUS-specific laptop controls such as keyboard RGB and battery charge limits.

Intro: Configure ASUS-specific laptop controls on Arch with asusctl and asusd. This workflow covers power profile separation, ASUS keyboard RGB, battery charge limits, a local asusd service workaround, and set-and-go usage when ASUS settings persist without keeping the daemon enabled all day.

Section: ASUS battery charge limit

Use the ASUS battery commands to inspect battery state, set a daily plugged-in charge limit, or perform a one-shot full charge before travel.

Code (bash):
# Inspect battery commands
asusctl battery --help
asusctl battery info

# Set daily plugged-in charge limit
asusctl battery limit 80

# One-shot full charge before travel
asusctl battery oneshot 100

- Static white keyboard color: asusctl aura effect static -c ffffff
- Daily battery preservation: asusctl battery limit 80
- Full charge before travel: asusctl battery oneshot 100
- Avoid custom fan curves unless there is a clear thermal reason.

title: Arch ASUS Laptop Controls
description: Use asusctl and asusd for ASUS-specific laptop controls such as keyboard RGB and battery charge limits.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch ASUS Laptop Controls

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Use asusctl and asusd for ASUS-specific laptop controls such as keyboard RGB and battery charge limits.

Intro: Configure ASUS-specific laptop controls on Arch with asusctl and asusd. This workflow covers power profile separation, ASUS keyboard RGB, battery charge limits, a local asusd service workaround, and set-and-go usage when ASUS settings persist without keeping the daemon enabled all day.

Section: ASUS set-and-go mode

If ASUS settings are rarely changed, asusd can be started only when applying settings and stopped afterward. Test this after a reboot because keyboard RGB may reset more often than the battery charge limit.

Code (bash):
# Apply ASUS preferences manually
sudo systemctl start asusd.service
asusctl aura effect static -c ffffff
asusctl battery limit 80
sudo systemctl stop asusd.service

Code (bash):
# Disable background startup if set-and-go is preferred
sudo systemctl disable asusd.service

# Start only when ASUS settings need to be changed again
sudo systemctl start asusd.service

- Arch, i3, sound, and powerprofilesctl work without asusd.
- asusd is needed only when asusctl needs to talk to ASUS-specific hardware controls.
- Keep asusd enabled if keyboard RGB resets after reboot or if ASUS controls are used often.
- Keep asusd disabled if the settings persist and the laptop is used in a fixed set-and-go style.

title: Nano Usage
description: Keyboard shortcuts for multiline selection, cut and paste, and fast movement in long files in nano.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Nano Usage

Category: Editors and IDEs

Description: Keyboard shortcuts for multiline selection, cut and paste, and fast movement in long files in nano.

Intro: Nano handles selection, cut, paste, and navigation entirely through keyboard shortcuts. This page is a quick reference for the shortcuts used most often during edits in long files.

Section: Shortcuts

Table:
Shortcut | Action
Ctrl+^ | Start selecting from cursor (same as Ctrl+Shift+6)
Ctrl+K | Cut selection, or delete the whole current line if nothing selected
Alt+6 | Copy selection without deleting
Ctrl+U | Paste most recently cut or copied text
Ctrl+V | Page down
Ctrl+Y | Page up
Alt+/ | Jump to end of file
Alt+\ | Jump to start of file
Ctrl+_ | Jump to specific line number

- After Ctrl+^ (start selection), expand the selection using arrow keys.
- After Ctrl+_, type the target line number and press Enter.
- Ctrl+K cuts the selection when one is active; otherwise it deletes the entire current line.

title: BigQuery to Cloud Storage Archive
description: Archive selected BigQuery tables to Cloud Storage as Parquet, then validate that the exports can be downloaded and read back.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: BigQuery to Cloud Storage Archive

Category: Cloud and Data Platforms

Description: Archive selected BigQuery tables to Cloud Storage as Parquet, then validate that the exports can be downloaded and read back.

Intro: Archive BigQuery tables to a Cloud Storage bucket as Parquet files. This is the flow used before deleting tables from BigQuery: export, confirm the objects exist, read them back locally, and only then remove the original tables. Assumes gcloud, the project venv, and ADC auth are already set up. See the gcloud and BigQuery setup page if not.

Section: Placeholders used below

Replace these before running any cell. The archive path shown here is the convention used throughout the rest of the page.

- YOUR_PROJECT_ID: GCP project ID
- YOUR_DATASET_ID: BigQuery dataset to archive
- YOUR_ARCHIVE_BUCKET: Cloud Storage bucket for archives
- TABLE_NAME_1, TABLE_NAME_2: tables to export
- YYYY-MM-DD: snapshot date for the archive path

Code (bash):
# variables to construct cloud storage paths:
gs://YOUR_ARCHIVE_BUCKET/bigquery/YOUR_DATASET_ID/TABLE_NAME/snapshot_date=YYYY-MM-DD/data.parquet

Resources:
- Google Cloud SDK and BigQuery Setup: /docs/gcloud-bigquery-project-setup

resources: Google Cloud SDK and BigQuery Setup: /docs/gcloud-bigquery-project-setup

Title: BigQuery to Cloud Storage Archive

Category: Cloud and Data Platforms

Description: Archive selected BigQuery tables to Cloud Storage as Parquet, then validate that the exports can be downloaded and read back.

Intro: Archive BigQuery tables to a Cloud Storage bucket as Parquet files. This is the flow used before deleting tables from BigQuery: export, confirm the objects exist, read them back locally, and only then remove the original tables. Assumes gcloud, the project venv, and ADC auth are already set up. See the gcloud and BigQuery setup page if not.

Section: Install Cloud Storage client

The BigQuery client alone is enough to submit an export job to Cloud Storage, but the Storage client is needed to create the bucket and to download the files back for validation.

Code (bash):
pip install google-cloud-storage

- Google Cloud SDK and BigQuery Setup: /docs/gcloud-bigquery-project-setup

sectionTitle: Create or reuse the archive bucket
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: BigQuery to Cloud Storage Archive

Category: Cloud and Data Platforms

Description: Archive selected BigQuery tables to Cloud Storage as Parquet, then validate that the exports can be downloaded and read back.

Intro: Archive BigQuery tables to a Cloud Storage bucket as Parquet files. This is the flow used before deleting tables from BigQuery: export, confirm the objects exist, read them back locally, and only then remove the original tables. Assumes gcloud, the project venv, and ADC auth are already set up. See the gcloud and BigQuery setup page if not.

Section: Create or reuse the archive bucket

Bucket names are globally unique across all of GCP. The bucket location should match the BigQuery dataset location so export, load, and external-table operations stay in the same region. Common values: EU, US, europe-west1.

Code (python):
from google.cloud import storage

project_id = "YOUR_PROJECT_ID"
bucket_name = "YOUR_ARCHIVE_BUCKET"
bucket_location = "EU"

storage_client = storage.Client(project=project_id)
bucket = storage_client.bucket(bucket_name)

print("Exists before create:", bucket.exists())

if not bucket.exists():
 bucket = storage_client.create_bucket(bucket_name, location=bucket_location)
 print("Created bucket:", bucket.name)
else:
 print("Bucket already exists:", bucket.name)

- Google Cloud SDK and BigQuery Setup: /docs/gcloud-bigquery-project-setup

title: BigQuery to Cloud Storage Archive
description: Archive selected BigQuery tables to Cloud Storage as Parquet, then validate that the exports can be downloaded and read back.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: BigQuery to Cloud Storage Archive

Category: Cloud and Data Platforms

Description: Archive selected BigQuery tables to Cloud Storage as Parquet, then validate that the exports can be downloaded and read back.

Intro: Archive BigQuery tables to a Cloud Storage bucket as Parquet files. This is the flow used before deleting tables from BigQuery: export, confirm the objects exist, read them back locally, and only then remove the original tables. Assumes gcloud, the project venv, and ADC auth are already set up. See the gcloud and BigQuery setup page if not.

Section: Export tables to Cloud Storage

Iterate over the tables and submit one extract job each. Each job writes a single Parquet file to the snapshot-dated path. For large tables, replace data.parquet with a wildcard pattern like data*.parquet to let BigQuery shard the output.

Code (python):
project_id = "YOUR_PROJECT_ID"
dataset_id = "YOUR_DATASET_ID"
bucket_name = "YOUR_ARCHIVE_BUCKET"
snapshot_date = "YYYY-MM-DD"

tables = [
 "TABLE_NAME_1",
 "TABLE_NAME_2",
]

bq_client = bigquery.Client(project=project_id)

for table_id in tables:
 source_table = f"{project_id}.{dataset_id}.{table_id}"
 destination_uri = (
 f"gs://{bucket_name}/bigquery/{dataset_id}/{table_id}"
 f"/snapshot_date={snapshot_date}/data.parquet"
 )

 extract_job = bq_client.extract_table(
 source_table,
 destination_uri,
 job_config=bigquery.job.ExtractJobConfig(destination_format="PARQUET"),
 )
 extract_job.result()

 print("Export completed")
 print(source_table)
 print(destination_uri)
 print()

Resources:
- Google Cloud SDK and BigQuery Setup: /docs/gcloud-bigquery-project-setup

resources: Google Cloud SDK and BigQuery Setup: /docs/gcloud-bigquery-project-setup

title: BigQuery to Cloud Storage Archive
description: Archive selected BigQuery tables to Cloud Storage as Parquet, then validate that the exports can be downloaded and read back.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: BigQuery to Cloud Storage Archive

Category: Cloud and Data Platforms

Description: Archive selected BigQuery tables to Cloud Storage as Parquet, then validate that the exports can be downloaded and read back.

Intro: Archive BigQuery tables to a Cloud Storage bucket as Parquet files. This is the flow used before deleting tables from BigQuery: export, confirm the objects exist, read them back locally, and only then remove the original tables. Assumes gcloud, the project venv, and ADC auth are already set up. See the gcloud and BigQuery setup page if not.

Section: Validate archived Parquet files

Download each archived Parquet back to /tmp, read it with pandas, and print its shape and first rows. This is a debug/validation step only.

Code (python):
from google.cloud import storage
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd

project_id = "YOUR_PROJECT_ID"
dataset_id = "YOUR_DATASET_ID"
bucket_name = "YOUR_ARCHIVE_BUCKET"
snapshot_date = "YYYY-MM-DD"
download_root = Path(f"/tmp/{dataset_id}_archive_validation")

tables = [
 "TABLE_NAME_1",
 "TABLE_NAME_2",
]

storage_client = storage.Client(project=project_id)
bucket = storage_client.bucket(bucket_name)
download_root.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

for table_id in tables:
 object_name = (
 f"bigquery/{dataset_id}/{table_id}"
 f"/snapshot_date={snapshot_date}/data.parquet"
 )
 local_path = download_root / f"{table_id}.parquet"

 blob = bucket.blob(object_name)
 blob.download_to_filename(str(local_path))

 df = pd.read_parquet(local_path)

 print("=" * 80)
 print(f"TABLE: {table_id}")
 print(f"OBJECT: gs://{bucket_name}/{object_name}")
 print(f"LOCAL FILE: {local_path}")
 print(f"SHAPE: {df.shape}")
 print(df.head(3))

Resources:
- Google Cloud SDK and BigQuery Setup: /docs/gcloud-bigquery-project-setup

resources: Google Cloud SDK and BigQuery Setup: /docs/gcloud-bigquery-project-setup

Source type: Doc

Title: BigQuery to Cloud Storage Archive

Category: Cloud and Data Platforms

Description: Archive selected BigQuery tables to Cloud Storage as Parquet, then validate that the exports can be downloaded and read back.

Intro: Archive BigQuery tables to a Cloud Storage bucket as Parquet files. This is the flow used before deleting tables from BigQuery: export, confirm the objects exist, read them back locally, and only then remove the original tables. Assumes gcloud, the project venv, and ADC auth are already set up. See the gcloud and BigQuery setup page if not.

Section: Deletion checklist

Only delete the original BigQuery tables after every item below is confirmed. Deletion itself can be done from the BigQuery UI.

- Every export job completed without error.
- The expected objects exist in Cloud Storage at the snapshot_date path.
- Each Parquet file downloaded and read back with pandas.
- Shape and sample rows look correct.
- No dashboard, scheduled query, app, or notebook still depends on the original BigQuery tables.

- Google Cloud SDK and BigQuery Setup: /docs/gcloud-bigquery-project-setup

title: SSH into a Local Network Device
description: Connect from a Linux host to another device on the same local network over SSH, using a named host alias in ~/.ssh/config.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: SSH into a Local Network Device

Category: Server and Deployment

Description: Connect from a Linux host to another device on the same local network over SSH, using a named host alias in ~/.ssh/config.

Intro: Connect from a Linux machine to another device on the same local network. In this case we will use a headless Ubuntu box whose IP is already known as the device to connect. Simply using an alias registry in ~/.ssh/config lets you connect with a short command instead of the full user and IP.

Section: Check existing SSH hosts

If the file does not exist, create it and set permissions. SSH ignores config and key files that are too open.

Code (bash):
mkdir -p ~/.ssh
touch ~/.ssh/config
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/config

title: SSH into a Local Network Device
description: Connect from a Linux host to another device on the same local network over SSH, using a named host alias in ~/.ssh/config.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: SSH into a Local Network Device

Category: Server and Deployment

Description: Connect from a Linux host to another device on the same local network over SSH, using a named host alias in ~/.ssh/config.

Intro: Connect from a Linux machine to another device on the same local network. In this case we will use a headless Ubuntu box whose IP is already known as the device to connect. Simply using an alias registry in ~/.ssh/config lets you connect with a short command instead of the full user and IP.

Section: Add the host to ~/.ssh/config

Open ~/.ssh/config and append the block below. Replace 192.168.1.5 with the target device IP and your user with the Linux username on that device. No extra file is needed for password login or the default SSH key.

Code (bash):
nano ~/.ssh/config

Code (bash):
Host local-ubuntu
 HostName 192.168.1.6
 User your-user

title: SSH into a Local Network Device
description: Connect from a Linux host to another device on the same local network over SSH, using a named host alias in ~/.ssh/config.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: SSH into a Local Network Device

Category: Server and Deployment

Description: Connect from a Linux host to another device on the same local network over SSH, using a named host alias in ~/.ssh/config.

Intro: Connect from a Linux machine to another device on the same local network. In this case we will use a headless Ubuntu box whose IP is already known as the device to connect. Simply using an alias registry in ~/.ssh/config lets you connect with a short command instead of the full user and IP.

Section: Connect

Once saved, connect using the alias.

Code (bash):
ssh local-ubuntu

The equivalent direct command without an alias:

Code (bash):
ssh myuser@192.168.1.5

On the first connection, SSH asks whether to trust the host fingerprint. Type yes if the IP and device are correct. If the target is configured for password login, the password prompt will show up.

After connection, move files when needed with scp command. Copy all files in a driectory with * or copy a specific file by filename.

title: SSH into a Local Network Device
description: Connect from a Linux host to another device on the same local network over SSH, using a named host alias in ~/.ssh/config.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: SSH into a Local Network Device

Category: Server and Deployment

Description: Connect from a Linux host to another device on the same local network over SSH, using a named host alias in ~/.ssh/config.

Intro: Connect from a Linux machine to another device on the same local network. In this case we will use a headless Ubuntu box whose IP is already known as the device to connect. Simply using an alias registry in ~/.ssh/config lets you connect with a short command instead of the full user and IP.

Section: Debug failed connections

Run SSH with verbose output to see where a failed connection breaks down.

Code (bash):
ssh -v local-ubuntu

If ssh reports 'No route to host', confirm basic reachability from the host machine and verify the target IP from the target machine itself.

Code (bash):
# From the host
ping 192.168.1.5

# From the target device
ip addr

If the target device is reachable on the network but SSH fails to connect, confirm the SSH server is installed and running on the target.

Code (bash):
# On the target Ubuntu device
sudo systemctl status ssh

# If not installed
sudo apt update
sudo apt install openssh-server
sudo systemctl enable --now ssh

title: Find Local Network IP
description: Find the IPv4 address of a Linux machine on the local network so another device can reach it over SSH.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Find Local Network IP

Category: Server and Deployment

Description: Find the IPv4 address of a Linux machine on the local network so another device can reach it over SSH.

Intro: This process how to find the LAN IPv4 address of a Linux machine and make sense of the shell outputs. A typically use case for this is to get the address to connect over SSH.

Section: Quickest check

Ask the kernel which interface and source address it would use to reach the public internet. The src field is the IP this machine presents on the LAN.

Code (bash):
ip route get 1.1.1.1

Example output:

Code (bash):
1.1.1.1 via 192.168.1.1 dev wlp4s0 src 192.168.1.4

The src 192.168.1.4 is the LAN IP. dev wlp4s0 is the active interface.

Resources:
- SSH into a local network device: /docs/ssh-local-device

title: Find Local Network IP
description: Find the IPv4 address of a Linux machine on the local network so another device can reach it over SSH.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Find Local Network IP

Category: Server and Deployment

Description: Find the IPv4 address of a Linux machine on the local network so another device can reach it over SSH.

Intro: This process how to find the LAN IPv4 address of a Linux machine and make sense of the shell outputs. A typically use case for this is to get the address to connect over SSH.

Section: Read full ip a output

When you need to see all interfaces run ip a and look for the inet line on the active interface.

Code (bash):
ip a

Example excerpt for a wired desktop:

Code (bash):
enp4s0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP>
 inet 192.168.1.6/24 brd 192.168.1.255 scope global dynamic noprefixroute enp4s0

Reading the line:

- enp4s0 is the wired Ethernet interface (en* = Ethernet, wl* = Wi-Fi, lo = loopback).
- UP means the interface is enabled, LOWER_UP means the physical link is active.
- scope global means it is a real network address, not loopback.
- dynamic means the IP was assigned by DHCP from the router.
- 192.168.1.6 is the LAN IP is the address to use for SSH from another machine.

Resources:
- SSH into a local network device: /docs/ssh-local-device

resources: SSH into a local network device: /docs/ssh-local-device

title: Find Local Network IP
description: Find the IPv4 address of a Linux machine on the local network so another device can reach it over SSH.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Find Local Network IP

Category: Server and Deployment

Description: Find the IPv4 address of a Linux machine on the local network so another device can reach it over SSH.

Intro: This process how to find the LAN IPv4 address of a Linux machine and make sense of the shell outputs. A typically use case for this is to get the address to connect over SSH.

Section: Interfaces to ignore

Several interfaces show up in ip a but are not the address you want for SSH from another machine.

- lo / 127.0.0.1 is a loopback. It points back to the same machine, unreachable from outside.
- docker0, br-..., veth... are Docker / container networks. Typically in 172.17–172.19.x.x.
- virbr0 is virtual machine bridge, often 192.168.122.x.

The address you want is the IPv4 on the real active LAN interface which is usually 192.168.1.x or similar, on en* (wired) or wl* (Wi-Fi).

Resources:
- SSH into a local network device: /docs/ssh-local-device

resources: SSH into a local network device: /docs/ssh-local-device

description: Find the IPv4 address of a Linux machine on the local network so another device can reach it over SSH.

Title: Find Local Network IP

Intro: This process how to find the LAN IPv4 address of a Linux machine and make sense of the shell outputs. A typically use case for this is to get the address to connect over SSH.

Section: Test reachability

From the other machine that will be doing the SSH, ping the discovered IP first to confirm basic reachability before trying to connect.

Code (bash):
ping 192.168.1.6
ssh your-user@192.168.1.6

title: CUDA Setup on Ubuntu 24.04
description: Verify CUDA availability, install the CUDA Toolkit compiler (nvcc), configure environment variables, and confirm GPU access from Python.
sectionTitle: Verify the driver and CUDA runtime
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: CUDA Setup on Ubuntu 24.04

Category: Server and Deployment

Description: Verify CUDA availability, install the CUDA Toolkit compiler (nvcc), configure environment variables, and confirm GPU access from Python.

Intro: Install and verify CUDA on Ubuntu 24.04 with an NVIDIA GPU and a working driver. The driver alone provides nvidia-smi and the CUDA runtime, but the Toolkit (nvcc and development tools) is a separate install. This page covers both and confirms GPU access from Python.

Section: Verify the driver and CUDA runtime

Confirm the GPU driver is working. The CUDA Version field shows the maximum runtime version supported by the driver, not proof that the CUDA Toolkit is installed.

Code (bash):
nvidia-smi

Expected output includes a line similar to:

Code (bash):
Driver Version: 590.xx CUDA Version: 13.1 GPU: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060

title: CUDA Setup on Ubuntu 24.04
description: Verify CUDA availability, install the CUDA Toolkit compiler (nvcc), configure environment variables, and confirm GPU access from Python.
sectionTitle: Check if the CUDA compiler exists
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: CUDA Setup on Ubuntu 24.04

Category: Server and Deployment

Description: Verify CUDA availability, install the CUDA Toolkit compiler (nvcc), configure environment variables, and confirm GPU access from Python.

Intro: Install and verify CUDA on Ubuntu 24.04 with an NVIDIA GPU and a working driver. The driver alone provides nvidia-smi and the CUDA runtime, but the Toolkit (nvcc and development tools) is a separate install. This page covers both and confirms GPU access from Python.

Section: Check if the CUDA compiler exists

If nvcc is missing, the CUDA Toolkit is not yet installed and the next steps apply.

Code (bash):
nvcc --version

When the Toolkit is missing, the output is:

Code (bash):
Command 'nvcc' not found

title: CUDA Setup on Ubuntu 24.04
description: Verify CUDA availability, install the CUDA Toolkit compiler (nvcc), configure environment variables, and confirm GPU access from Python.

Source type: Doc

Title: CUDA Setup on Ubuntu 24.04

Category: Server and Deployment

Description: Verify CUDA availability, install the CUDA Toolkit compiler (nvcc), configure environment variables, and confirm GPU access from Python.

Intro: Install and verify CUDA on Ubuntu 24.04 with an NVIDIA GPU and a working driver. The driver alone provides nvidia-smi and the CUDA runtime, but the Toolkit (nvcc and development tools) is a separate install. This page covers both and confirms GPU access from Python.

Section: Add the NVIDIA CUDA repository

On Ubuntu 24.04, CUDA packages require the NVIDIA CUDA apt repository. Add the keyring and refresh the package index.

Code (bash):
wget https://developer.download.nvidia.com/compute/cuda/repos/ubuntu2404/x86_64/cuda-keyring_1.1-1_all.deb
sudo dpkg -i cuda-keyring_1.1-1_all.deb
sudo apt update

Install the Toolkit. This brings in nvcc and supporting development tools.

title: CUDA Setup on Ubuntu 24.04
description: Verify CUDA availability, install the CUDA Toolkit compiler (nvcc), configure environment variables, and confirm GPU access from Python.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: CUDA Setup on Ubuntu 24.04

Category: Server and Deployment

Description: Verify CUDA availability, install the CUDA Toolkit compiler (nvcc), configure environment variables, and confirm GPU access from Python.

Intro: Install and verify CUDA on Ubuntu 24.04 with an NVIDIA GPU and a working driver. The driver alone provides nvidia-smi and the CUDA runtime, but the Toolkit (nvcc and development tools) is a separate install. This page covers both and confirms GPU access from Python.

Section: Configure environment variables

Append the CUDA bin and lib64 directories to PATH and LD_LIBRARY_PATH. This makes nvcc available on the command line and lets the dynamic linker find the CUDA shared libraries.

Code (bash):
echo 'export PATH=/usr/local/cuda-13.1/bin:$PATH' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/local/cuda-13.1/lib64:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH' >> ~/.bashrc

Reload the shell configuration and clear the command hash table so the new nvcc path resolves immediately.

Code (bash):
source ~/.bashrc
hash -r

Confirm the install.

Code (bash):
nvcc --version

Expected output:

Code (bash):
Cuda compilation tools, release 13.1

title: CUDA Setup on Ubuntu 24.04
description: Verify CUDA availability, install the CUDA Toolkit compiler (nvcc), configure environment variables, and confirm GPU access from Python.

Source type: Doc

Title: CUDA Setup on Ubuntu 24.04

Category: Server and Deployment

Description: Verify CUDA availability, install the CUDA Toolkit compiler (nvcc), configure environment variables, and confirm GPU access from Python.

Intro: Install and verify CUDA on Ubuntu 24.04 with an NVIDIA GPU and a working driver. The driver alone provides nvidia-smi and the CUDA runtime, but the Toolkit (nvcc and development tools) is a separate install. This page covers both and confirms GPU access from Python.

Section: Verify CUDA from Python

Activate the project virtual environment and run a quick check that PyTorch sees the GPU.

Code (bash):
source venv/bin/activate

Code (python):
import torch

print("torch version:", torch.__version__)
print("cuda available:", torch.cuda.is_available())
print("cuda device count:", torch.cuda.device_count())

if torch.cuda.is_available():
 print("gpu name:", torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))
 print("cuda capability:", torch.cuda.get_device_capability(0))

Code (bash):
python3 cuda_test.py

Expected output:

Code (bash):
torch version: 2.10.0+cu126
cuda available: True
cuda device count: 1
gpu name: NVIDIA ...
cuda capability: (8, 6)

title: Windows 11 VM on Ubuntu with KVM/QEMU
description: UEFI based Windows 11 virtual machine on an Ubuntu host using QEMU/KVM, libvirt, and virt-manager.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Windows 11 VM on Ubuntu with KVM/QEMU

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: UEFI based Windows 11 virtual machine on an Ubuntu host using QEMU/KVM, libvirt, and virt-manager.

Intro: Ubuntu virtualization stack for running Windows 11 as a guest: QEMU/KVM, libvirt, virt-manager, OVMF (UEFI), and TPM 2.0. Both UEFI firmware and TPM are required by Windows 11 and provided by this stack.

Section: Verify CPU virtualization

Confirm the host CPU supports hardware virtualization. Output greater than 0 means the CPU supports it. If the count is 0, enable Intel VT-x or AMD-V in BIOS first.

Code (bash):
egrep -c '(vmx|svm)' /proc/cpuinfo

Resources:
- Windows 11 ISO: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/software-download/windows11
- Arch Windows VM with KVM/QEMU: /docs/arch-windows-vm

title: Windows 11 VM on Ubuntu with KVM/QEMU
description: UEFI based Windows 11 virtual machine on an Ubuntu host using QEMU/KVM, libvirt, and virt-manager.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Windows 11 VM on Ubuntu with KVM/QEMU

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: UEFI based Windows 11 virtual machine on an Ubuntu host using QEMU/KVM, libvirt, and virt-manager.

Intro: Ubuntu virtualization stack for running Windows 11 as a guest: QEMU/KVM, libvirt, virt-manager, OVMF (UEFI), and TPM 2.0. Both UEFI firmware and TPM are required by Windows 11 and provided by this stack.

Section: Install the KVM stack

Code (bash):
sudo apt update
sudo apt install qemu-kvm libvirt-daemon-system libvirt-clients bridge-utils virt-manager
sudo systemctl enable libvirtd
sudo systemctl start libvirtd
sudo usermod -aG libvirt $USER

Log out and back in after usermod for group membership to take effect.

Verify KVM kernel modules are loaded. Output should include kvm_intel or kvm_amd depending on the CPU.

Code (bash):
lsmod | grep kvm

Resources:
- Windows 11 ISO: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/software-download/windows11
- Arch Windows VM with KVM/QEMU: /docs/arch-windows-vm

title: Windows 11 VM on Ubuntu with KVM/QEMU
description: UEFI based Windows 11 virtual machine on an Ubuntu host using QEMU/KVM, libvirt, and virt-manager.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Windows 11 VM on Ubuntu with KVM/QEMU

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: UEFI based Windows 11 virtual machine on an Ubuntu host using QEMU/KVM, libvirt, and virt-manager.

Intro: Ubuntu virtualization stack for running Windows 11 as a guest: QEMU/KVM, libvirt, virt-manager, OVMF (UEFI), and TPM 2.0. Both UEFI firmware and TPM are required by Windows 11 and provided by this stack.

Section: Recommended VM configuration

Tested working configuration on a 12 core host with 32 GB RAM:

- Memory: 16 GB
- vCPU: 6
- Disk: 100 GB, qcow2 (default)
- Firmware: UEFI / OVMF
- TPM: 2.0 (required for Windows 11)
- CPU model: host-passthrough
- Network: default libvirt NAT

Resources:
- Windows 11 ISO: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/software-download/windows11
- Arch Windows VM with KVM/QEMU: /docs/arch-windows-vm

title: Multiple Python Versions on Ubuntu
description: Install several Python versions side by side on Ubuntu using the deadsnakes PPA, then create a venv pinned to a specific version.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Multiple Python Versions on Ubuntu

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Install several Python versions side by side on Ubuntu using the deadsnakes PPA, then create a venv pinned to a specific version.

Intro: Ubuntu ships with one Python version per release. The deadsnakes PPA provides additional Python versions as separate packages so multiple versions can coexist. Each version installs as its own binary (python3.9, python3.10, etc.) and can be used to create version pinned virtual environments.

Section: Add the deadsnakes PPA

Code (bash):
sudo apt install software-properties-common
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:deadsnakes/ppa
sudo apt update

title: Multiple Python Versions on Ubuntu
description: Install several Python versions side by side on Ubuntu using the deadsnakes PPA, then create a venv pinned to a specific version.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Multiple Python Versions on Ubuntu

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Install several Python versions side by side on Ubuntu using the deadsnakes PPA, then create a venv pinned to a specific version.

Intro: Ubuntu ships with one Python version per release. The deadsnakes PPA provides additional Python versions as separate packages so multiple versions can coexist. Each version installs as its own binary (python3.9, python3.10, etc.) and can be used to create version pinned virtual environments.

Section: Install Python versions

Install only the versions actually needed. Each one installs alongside the system Python without replacing it.

Code (bash):
sudo apt install python3.9 python3.10 python3.11 python3.12 python3.13

Verify the installed binaries:

Code (bash):
ls /usr/bin/python3*

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Intro: Ubuntu ships with one Python version per release. The deadsnakes PPA provides additional Python versions as separate packages so multiple versions can coexist. Each version installs as its own binary (python3.9, python3.10, etc.) and can be used to create version pinned virtual environments.

Section: Install venv support

On Ubuntu, the venv module ships in a separate package per version. Install the matching venv package for each Python version that needs to create virtual environments.

Code (bash):
sudo apt install python3.9-venv python3.10-venv python3.11-venv python3.12-venv

Title: Multiple Python Versions on Ubuntu

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Install several Python versions side by side on Ubuntu using the deadsnakes PPA, then create a venv pinned to a specific version.

Intro: Ubuntu ships with one Python version per release. The deadsnakes PPA provides additional Python versions as separate packages so multiple versions can coexist. Each version installs as its own binary (python3.9, python3.10, etc.) and can be used to create version pinned virtual environments.

Section: Create a venv with a specific version

Call the version specific binary to create the venv. The resulting environment is pinned to that interpreter.

Code (bash):
python3.11 -m venv myenv
source myenv/bin/activate
python --version
pip install --upgrade pip

title: Install NVM on Ubuntu
description: Install Node Version Manager on Ubuntu, then install and switch between Node.js versions per user without touching system packages.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Install NVM on Ubuntu

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Install Node Version Manager on Ubuntu, then install and switch between Node.js versions per user without touching system packages.

Intro: NVM (Node Version Manager) installs and manages multiple Node.js versions per user. The apt nodejs package installs system-wide, is often outdated, and makes version switching difficult. NVM avoids both problems and is the standard for modern JS development.

Section: Install NVM

The install script clones the NVM repo into ~/.nvm and adds initialization lines to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc. Check the nvm-sh/nvm repo for the current release and replace v0.40.4 below if a newer version is out.

Code (bash):
curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.4/install.sh | bash

Resources:
- nvm-sh/nvm on GitHub: https://github.com/nvm-sh/nvm

title: Install NVM on Ubuntu
description: Install Node Version Manager on Ubuntu, then install and switch between Node.js versions per user without touching system packages.
text:

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Install Node Version Manager on Ubuntu, then install and switch between Node.js versions per user without touching system packages.

Intro: NVM (Node Version Manager) installs and manages multiple Node.js versions per user. The apt nodejs package installs system-wide, is often outdated, and makes version switching difficult. NVM avoids both problems and is the standard for modern JS development.

Section: Activate NVM

Either restart the terminal, or reload the shell config in the current session.

Code (bash):
source ~/.bashrc

Verify the install.

Code (bash):
nvm --version

Title: Install NVM on Ubuntu

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Install Node Version Manager on Ubuntu, then install and switch between Node.js versions per user without touching system packages.

Intro: NVM (Node Version Manager) installs and manages multiple Node.js versions per user. The apt nodejs package installs system-wide, is often outdated, and makes version switching difficult. NVM avoids both problems and is the standard for modern JS development.

Section: Install Node.js

Install the current LTS, set it as the active version, and pin it as the default for new shells.

Code (bash):
nvm install --lts
nvm use --lts
nvm alias default lts/*

Confirm node and npm are available.

Code (bash):
node -v
npm -v

Resources:
- nvm-sh/nvm on GitHub: https://github.com/nvm-sh/nvm

title: Install NVM on Ubuntu
description: Install Node Version Manager on Ubuntu, then install and switch between Node.js versions per user without touching system packages.

Source type: Doc

Title: Install NVM on Ubuntu

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Install Node Version Manager on Ubuntu, then install and switch between Node.js versions per user without touching system packages.

Intro: NVM (Node Version Manager) installs and manages multiple Node.js versions per user. The apt nodejs package installs system-wide, is often outdated, and makes version switching difficult. NVM avoids both problems and is the standard for modern JS development.

Section: Manage versions

Code (bash):
nvm install 20 # install a specific major version
nvm use 20 # switch the current shell to that version
nvm alias default 20 # set the default for new shells
nvm ls # list installed versions
nvm uninstall 18 # remove a version

Resources:
- nvm-sh/nvm on GitHub: https://github.com/nvm-sh/nvm

Piping a remote script straight into bash should only be done for trusted sources. To inspect the script before running it, download first.

title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Overview

- Use brightnessctl for the laptop screen exposed under /sys/class/backlight.
- Use ddcutil for external monitors that support DDC/CI brightness control.
- Use redshift -O for manual blue light reduction across active X11 displays.
- Use redshift -x to reset the screen color temperature back to normal.
- Create one all-bright command to set laptop and external monitor brightness together.
- Keep brightness and night mode separate so they can be adjusted independently.

title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Install required tools

Install brightnessctl for the laptop backlight, ddcutil and i2c-tools for external monitor brightness, and redshift for manual night mode.

Code (bash):
sudo pacman -S brightnessctl ddcutil i2c-tools redshift

title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Check laptop backlight device

The laptop screen is controlled through a backlight device. The device name depends on the machine and GPU. Common examples include nvidia_0, intel_backlight, and amdgpu_bl0.

Code (bash):
ls /sys/class/backlight
brightnessctl

Use the detected device name when setting brightness explicitly. Replace nvidia_0 with the device name shown on your system.

Code (bash):
brightnessctl -d nvidia_0 set 40%

title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Enable external monitor DDC access

External monitors are controlled through DDC/CI over i2c. Load the i2c-dev module before using ddcutil.

Code (bash):
sudo modprobe i2c-dev
ddcutil detect

title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Map detected displays

- Use ddcutil detect to find the external monitor display numbers.
- External monitor 1 is usually controlled with ddcutil --display 1.
- External monitor 2 is usually controlled with ddcutil --display 2.
- The laptop display may appear as an invalid DDC display because laptop panels usually do not support DDC/CI.
- Use brightnessctl for the laptop display.
- Use ddcutil for each detected external monitor.

Code (bash):
ddcutil detect

title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Check external monitor brightness

VCP code 10 is the standard DDC/CI brightness control. Use getvcp before changing brightness to confirm monitor support.

Code (bash):
ddcutil --display 1 getvcp 10
ddcutil --display 2 getvcp 10

title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Source type: Doc

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Set external monitor brightness manually

Set each external monitor brightness with ddcutil. The value is usually between 0 and 100. Add or remove display lines depending on how many external monitors are detected.

Code (bash):
ddcutil --display 1 setvcp 10 40
ddcutil --display 2 setvcp 10 40

title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Create all-bright command

Create one command that sets the laptop screen and external monitors to the same brightness value. Replace nvidia_0 with the laptop backlight device detected on your system. Add or remove ddcutil display lines depending on the number of external monitors.

Code (bash):
nano ~/.local/bin/all-bright

Code (bash):
#!/usr/bin/env bash

VALUE="$1"

if [ -z "$VALUE" ]; then
 echo "Usage: all-bright 40"
 exit 1
fi

brightnessctl -d nvidia_0 set "$VALUE%"

ddcutil --display 1 setvcp 10 "$VALUE"
ddcutil --display 2 setvcp 10 "$VALUE"

Code (bash):
chmod +x ~/.local/bin/all-bright

title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.
sectionTitle: Make local scripts available in shell
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Make local scripts available in shell

The ~/.local/bin folder must be in PATH so all-bright can be called from any terminal.

Code (bash):
grep -q 'HOME/.local/bin' ~/.bashrc || echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
command -v all-bright

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
