Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Manual night mode with redshift

Use redshift in one-shot mode for manual blue light reduction. This changes color temperature across active X11 displays and can be reset manually.

Code (bash):
redshift -O 3500
redshift -x

title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Redshift temperature reference

- redshift -O 4500 gives a mild warm screen.
- redshift -O 4000 gives a moderate warm screen.
- redshift -O 3500 gives a clear night mode look.
- redshift -O 3000 gives a stronger orange night mode look.
- redshift -x resets the color temperature back to normal.

title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Daily usage

Use these commands manually depending on the time of day and room lighting.

Code (bash):
# Set all screens to a comfortable daytime brightness
all-bright 70

# Set all screens to a lower evening brightness
all-bright 40

# Enable manual night mode with reduced blue light
redshift -O 3500

# Disable night mode and return colors to normal
redshift -x

# Evening setup with lower brightness and warmer color temperature
all-bright 40
redshift -O 3500

# Daytime setup with higher brightness and normal color temperature
all-bright 70
redshift -x

title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode
description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Arch Display Brightness and Night Mode

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Manual brightness and blue light control for an Arch Linux i3 multi-monitor setup.

Intro: This setup controls laptop brightness with brightnessctl, external monitor hardware brightness with ddcutil, and blue light reduction with redshift. It is useful for an Arch Linux i3 workstation with one laptop screen and one or more external monitors.

Section: Troubleshooting

- If brightnessctl only changes the laptop screen, this is expected.
- If the explicit brightnessctl device does not exist, check the available device with ls /sys/class/backlight.
- If ddcutil detect does not show external monitors, load i2c-dev with sudo modprobe i2c-dev.
- If ddcutil works with sudo but not as normal user, add the user to the i2c group and log out and back in.
- If redshift changes color but not brightness, this is expected because redshift controls color temperature, not hardware brightness.
- If redshift does not affect all monitors, confirm the session is running on X11 and not Wayland.

Code (bash):
ls /sys/class/backlight
sudo modprobe i2c-dev
sudo usermod -aG i2c "$USER"
echo "$XDG_SESSION_TYPE"

title: Linux Service Operations
description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Linux Service Operations

Category: System Operations

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Overview

- Use systemctl to control systemd-managed services.
- Use daemon-reload after creating, editing, or deleting systemd unit files.
- Use restart for app services after code or environment changes.
- Use nginx -t and systemctl reload nginx after Nginx config changes.
- Use journalctl for systemd service logs.
- Use docker compose logs for container application logs.
- Use cron for simple time-based shell commands.
- Use Celery for application background jobs, retries, queues, and scheduled app tasks.

title: Linux Service Operations
description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Linux Service Operations

Category: System Operations

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Mental model

Code (bash):
Linux kernel
 -> systemd
 -> services
 -> nginx
 -> docker
 -> ssh
 -> cron or cronie
 -> custom app services

systemctl
 -> command tool used to control systemd

journalctl
 -> command tool used to read logs collected by systemd-journald

- systemd is the service manager daemon.
- systemctl is the command-line control tool.
- journalctl is the command-line log reader.
- Nginx, Docker, cron, and custom apps can all run as services managed by systemd.

title: Linux Service Operations
description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Linux Service Operations

Category: System Operations

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Check PID 1

PID 1 is usually systemd on modern Arch and Ubuntu systems. It may appear as /sbin/init because that path points to systemd.

Code (bash):
ps -p 1 -o pid,comm,args
ls -l /sbin/init
readlink -f /sbin/init

Example output:

Code (bash):
PID COMMAND COMMAND
1 systemd /sbin/init

/sbin/init -> ../lib/systemd/systemd
/usr/lib/systemd/systemd

title: Linux Service Operations
description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Linux Service Operations

Category: System Operations

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Common systemctl commands

Code (bash):
systemctl status nginx
systemctl status docker
systemctl status my-app.service

sudo systemctl start my-app.service
sudo systemctl stop my-app.service
sudo systemctl restart my-app.service

sudo systemctl enable my-app.service
sudo systemctl disable my-app.service

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl reset-failed

Table:
Command | Use
status | Show service state, recent logs, and main process
start | Start a stopped service
stop | Stop a running service
restart | Stop and start the service process
reload | Ask a service to re-read its own config if supported
enable | Start service automatically on boot
disable | Prevent service from starting on boot
daemon-reload | Reload systemd unit definitions
reset-failed | Clear old failed-state records

title: Linux Service Operations
description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Linux Service Operations

Category: System Operations

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: daemon-reload, restart, and reload

- daemon-reload refreshes systemd unit definitions. It does not restart running services.
- restart restarts the actual service process.
- reload asks a running service to re-read its own config if the service supports reload.

Code (bash):
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart my-app.service
sudo systemctl reload nginx

Use both daemon-reload and restart after changing a .service file.

Code (bash):
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/my-app.service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart my-app.service
sudo systemctl status my-app.service

Use only restart when the app code changed but the .service file did not change.

Code (bash):
sudo systemctl restart my-app.service
sudo systemctl status my-app.service

title: Linux Service Operations
description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Linux Service Operations

Category: System Operations

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Create a systemd service

Create a service file under /etc/systemd/system for custom application services.

Code (bash):
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/my-app.service

Example service file:

Code (bash):
[Unit]
 Description=My App Service
 After=network.target

 [Service]
 WorkingDirectory=/home/ubuntu/my-app
 ExecStart=/home/ubuntu/my-app/.venv/bin/python app.py
 Restart=always
 RestartSec=5
 User=ubuntu
 EnvironmentFile=/home/ubuntu/my-app/.env

 [Install]
 WantedBy=multi-user.target

Load the new definition, start it, enable it on boot, and inspect status.

Code (bash):
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl start my-app.service
sudo systemctl enable my-app.service
sudo systemctl status my-app.service

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Update a systemd service

If the service file changes, reload systemd definitions and restart the service process.

Code (bash):
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/my-app.service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart my-app.service
sudo systemctl status my-app.service

If only application code changed, restart the service without daemon-reload.

Code (bash):
sudo systemctl restart my-app.service
sudo systemctl status my-app.service

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Remove a systemd service

Stop the service, disable boot startup, confirm the unit file path, remove the unit file, reload systemd definitions, and clear failed state if needed.

Code (bash):
sudo systemctl stop service-name.service
sudo systemctl disable service-name.service
sudo systemctl cat service-name.service
sudo rm /etc/systemd/system/service-name.service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl reset-failed
systemctl status service-name.service

Expected final output:

Code (bash):
Unit service-name.service could not be found.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Remove multiple custom services

Use one command per phase when removing a group of related services.

Code (bash):
sudo systemctl stop app-backend.service app-worker.service app-beat.service app-frontend.service

sudo systemctl disable app-backend.service app-worker.service app-beat.service app-frontend.service

sudo rm /etc/systemd/system/app-backend.service
sudo rm /etc/systemd/system/app-worker.service
sudo rm /etc/systemd/system/app-beat.service
sudo rm /etc/systemd/system/app-frontend.service

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl reset-failed

Confirm no matching units remain.

Code (bash):
systemctl list-unit-files | grep app
systemctl list-units --all | grep app

Section: Nginx config locations

Code (bash):
/etc/nginx/nginx.conf
/etc/nginx/sites-available/
/etc/nginx/sites-enabled/

- sites-available stores site config files.
- sites-enabled stores symlinks to active site configs.

Section: Remove an Nginx site config

Remove the enabled symlink first, then remove the available config file. Test before reloading Nginx.

ls -l /etc/nginx/sites-enabled | grep example-site

sudo rm -f /etc/nginx/sites-available/example-site.conf

sudo systemctl reload nginx

nginx: the configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf syntax is ok
nginx: configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf test is successful

sectionTitle: Why Nginx is used in production
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Linux Service Operations

Category: System Operations

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Why Nginx is used in production

In development, apps are often accessed directly on localhost ports. In production, users usually access Nginx on ports 80 and 443, then Nginx forwards traffic to internal app ports.

Code (bash):
Development:

Browser
 -> http://localhost:3000
 -> http://localhost:8000

Production:

Browser
 -> https://example.com
 -> Nginx on port 443
 -> frontend app on localhost or Docker
 -> backend app on localhost or Docker

- Nginx handles public ports 80 and 443.
- Nginx can handle HTTPS certificates.
- Nginx routes domains and paths to the correct app.
- Nginx keeps app ports hidden from the public internet.
- Nginx can reload config without fully restarting the service.

Section: AWS ALB and Nginx

- AWS Application Load Balancer can replace Nginx as the public reverse proxy in many AWS-native setups.
- ALB is useful with multiple EC2 instances, ECS services, target groups, health checks, and autoscaling.
- Nginx is often simpler for a single EC2 instance.

Code (bash):
Single EC2 pattern:

Browser
 -> Nginx on EC2
 -> app services or Docker containers

AWS ALB pattern:

Browser
 -> AWS Application Load Balancer
 -> EC2 instance
 -> ECS task
 -> target group

text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Linux Service Operations

Category: System Operations

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Kernel threads in ps output

Processes shown inside square brackets are usually kernel threads, not normal user services.

Code (bash):
root 1 0.0 0.0 26452 15516 ? Ss Apr30 0:03 /sbin/init
root 2 0.0 0.0 0 0 ? S Apr30 0:00 [kthreadd]
root 3 0.0 0.0 0 0 ? S Apr30 0:00 [pool_workqueue]
root 15 0.0 0.0 0 0 ? S Apr30 0:01 [ksoftirqd/0]

- PID 1 is usually systemd or /sbin/init.
- PID 2 is kthreadd, the kernel thread manager.
- kworker entries are kernel worker threads.
- ksoftirqd entries are kernel interrupt handling workers.

Section: journalctl and systemd-journald

- systemd-journald is the background log collection daemon.
- journalctl is the command used to read logs.
- Use journalctl for services managed directly by systemd.

Code (bash):
journalctl -u nginx
journalctl -u docker
journalctl -u my-app.service

journalctl -u my-app.service -f
journalctl -u my-app.service -n 100
journalctl -u my-app.service --since "1 hour ago"
journalctl -u my-app.service --since today

journalctl -b
journalctl -u my-app.service -b

Check journald itself:

Code (bash):
systemctl status systemd-journald
ps aux | grep '[s]ystemd-journald'

Section: Docker logs and journal logs

- journalctl -u docker shows Docker daemon logs.
- docker compose logs shows logs from application containers.
- Use Docker logs for app exceptions inside containers.
- Use journalctl -u docker for Docker engine, network, image, and daemon problems.

Code (bash):
systemd
 -> docker.service
 -> Docker daemon
 -> containers
 -> backend app
 -> frontend app

Code (bash):
journalctl -u docker -f

docker compose logs -f backend
docker compose logs -f frontend

docker compose logs --tail=100 backend
docker compose logs --tail=100 frontend

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Cron and cronie

- cron is a time-based command scheduler.
- cronie is a common cron implementation on Arch.
- crontab edits the current user's scheduled jobs.
- Cron uses the system's local timezone by default.

Code (bash):
sudo pacman -S cronie
sudo systemctl enable --now cronie
systemctl status cronie

crontab -e
crontab -l

Use nano if vi is missing.

Code (bash):
EDITOR=nano crontab -e

echo 'export EDITOR=nano' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

Title: Linux Service Operations

Category: System Operations

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Cron time format

Code (bash):
minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week command

Example: run a Python script every day at 03:00.

Code (bash):
0 3 * * * /home/example/project/.venv/bin/python /home/example/project/scripts/daily_job.py

Check local system time and timezone.

Code (bash):
date
timedatectl
timedatectl | grep "Time zone"

Set timezone if needed.

Code (bash):
sudo timedatectl set-timezone Europe/Istanbul

Source type: Doc

Title: Linux Service Operations

Category: System Operations

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Cron logging pattern

Redirect command output to a log file when running scripts from cron. This keeps print output and errors easy to inspect.

Code (bash):
mkdir -p /home/example/project/logs

Code (bash):
0 3 * * * cd /home/example/project && /home/example/project/.venv/bin/python scripts/daily_job.py >> logs/daily_job.log 2>&1

- >> appends normal output to the log file.
- 2>&1 sends error output to the same log file.

Code (bash):
tail -f /home/example/project/logs/daily_job.log

journalctl -u cronie --since today

title: Linux Service Operations
description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Linux Service Operations

Category: System Operations

Description: Operate systemd services, Nginx configs, process IDs, journal logs, Docker logs, and cron jobs on Linux servers and Arch workstations.

Intro: This page is a quick operational reference for Linux service management and server maintenance. We cover systemd and systemctl, service creation and removal, Nginx site cleanup, PID checks, journalctl, Docker logs, cron, and cron versus Celery.

Section: Cron versus Celery

Table:
Use case | Better tool
Run one Python script every day | cron
Run backup or cleanup commands | cron
Send verification email after user registration | Celery
Process uploads or reports in the background | Celery
Retry failed external API work | Celery
Schedule app-level tasks with workers | Celery beat

Code (bash):
cron
 At this time, run this shell command.

Celery
 Put this app task into a background queue.

Celery beat
 At this time, put this app task into the Celery queue.

Redis
 Broker where Celery tasks wait.

Celery worker
 Process that consumes queued tasks.

For web apps, slow or retryable work should usually move out of the request and into a background worker.

Code (bash):
User registers
 -> backend creates user
 -> backend queues send_verification_email task
 -> backend returns response quickly

Celery worker
 -> sends email separately
 -> retries if needed

title: LibreOffice Reference
description: Use LibreOffice on Arch Linux for writing documents, editing office files, opening PDFs visually, and exporting documents to PDF.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: LibreOffice Reference

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Use LibreOffice on Arch Linux for writing documents, editing office files, opening PDFs visually, and exporting documents to PDF.

Intro: LibreOffice is the main office suite on Linux. It provides separate apps for documents, spreadsheets, presentations, drawings, formulas, and database files. The same libreoffice command can launch each app with a different parameter.

Section: Install LibreOffice

Install LibreOffice from the Arch repositories. libreoffice-fresh is usually the best default choice for a normal desktop setup.

Code (bash):
sudo pacman -S libreoffice-fresh

title: LibreOffice Reference
description: Use LibreOffice on Arch Linux for writing documents, editing office files, opening PDFs visually, and exporting documents to PDF.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: LibreOffice Reference

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Use LibreOffice on Arch Linux for writing documents, editing office files, opening PDFs visually, and exporting documents to PDF.

Intro: LibreOffice is the main office suite on Linux. It provides separate apps for documents, spreadsheets, presentations, drawings, formulas, and database files. The same libreoffice command can launch each app with a different parameter.

Section: Launch office apps

LibreOffice is the host suite. Each app can be launched from the same libreoffice command by passing the target app name.

Table:
Command | App | Use case
libreoffice --writer | Writer | Text documents, letters, notes, translations, PDF export
libreoffice --calc | Calc | Spreadsheets, tables, CSV files
libreoffice --impress | Impress | Presentations and slide decks
libreoffice --draw | Draw | Drawings, diagrams, and visual PDF edits
libreoffice --math | Math | Formula documents
libreoffice --base | Base | Database-style documents

Title: LibreOffice Reference

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Use LibreOffice on Arch Linux for writing documents, editing office files, opening PDFs visually, and exporting documents to PDF.

Intro: LibreOffice is the main office suite on Linux. It provides separate apps for documents, spreadsheets, presentations, drawings, formulas, and database files. The same libreoffice command can launch each app with a different parameter.

Section: Open a file directly

A file can be opened directly. LibreOffice chooses the matching app based on the file type.

Code (bash):
libreoffice document.odt
libreoffice document.docx
libreoffice spreadsheet.xlsx
libreoffice presentation.pptx

Use Writer for .odt and .docx files. .odt is OpenDocument Text, the native LibreOffice Writer format.

Title: LibreOffice Reference

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Use LibreOffice on Arch Linux for writing documents, editing office files, opening PDFs visually, and exporting documents to PDF.

Intro: LibreOffice is the main office suite on Linux. It provides separate apps for documents, spreadsheets, presentations, drawings, formulas, and database files. The same libreoffice command can launch each app with a different parameter.

Section: Export a document to PDF

From the LibreOffice interface, use File, Export As, then Export Directly as PDF. This is the safest method when you want to review the document before creating the final PDF.

For command-line export, use headless mode. This creates a PDF in the current directory.

Code (bash):
libreoffice --headless --convert-to pdf document.odt

Title: LibreOffice Reference

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Use LibreOffice on Arch Linux for writing documents, editing office files, opening PDFs visually, and exporting documents to PDF.

Intro: LibreOffice is the main office suite on Linux. It provides separate apps for documents, spreadsheets, presentations, drawings, formulas, and database files. The same libreoffice command can launch each app with a different parameter.

Section: Edit an existing PDF visually

Use Draw when the task is to open a PDF and make visual changes. This is useful for small text changes, moving objects, adding text boxes, or adjusting document pages visually.

Code (bash):
libreoffice --draw document.pdf

- PDF editing is not the same as editing a Word document.
- Complex PDFs may open with shifted layout or split text boxes.
- For scanned PDFs, the page may behave like an image instead of editable text.
- After editing, export the result back to PDF from File, Export As.

Title: VS Code Workspace Excludes

Category: Editors and IDEs

Description: Hide generated folders from the VS Code Explorer and exclude them from search results.

Intro: This document shows how to use a project-level VS Code settings file to hide generated folders such as __pycache__, .next, and node_modules from the Explorer sidebar and normal search results.

Section: Create the workspace settings file

Create a .vscode directory inside the project root. VS Code reads settings.json from this folder automatically when the project is opened.

Code (bash):
mkdir -p .vscode
nano .vscode/settings.json

Title: VS Code Workspace Excludes

Category: Editors and IDEs

Description: Hide generated folders from the VS Code Explorer and exclude them from search results.

Intro: This document shows how to use a project-level VS Code settings file to hide generated folders such as __pycache__, .next, and node_modules from the Explorer sidebar and normal search results.

Section: What each rule does

Table:
Setting | Purpose
files.exclude | Hides matching files and folders from the Explorer sidebar.
search.exclude | Prevents matching files and folders from appearing in normal search results.
**/__pycache__ | Matches Python bytecode cache folders at any depth.
**/.next | Matches Next.js build output folders at any depth.
**/node_modules | Matches Node.js dependency folders at any depth.

Copy the same .vscode/settings.json file into other projects when the same behavior is needed.
If settings.json already exists, merge the files.exclude and search.exclude blocks into the existing JSON instead of replacing the whole file.

title: Python Import Path and Package Checks
description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: Core idea

Python imports are resolved from the paths listed in sys.path. In a backend project, the most important path is usually the project root, the folder that contains the application entry file.
If the project root is on sys.path, Python can import folders under that root. For example, from services.auth_service import login_user resolves services from the project folder.
__init__.py is not a magic fix for wrong paths. It changes a folder from a namespace package candidate into a regular package.

Table:
Concept | Meaning
Module | A Python file, for example auth_service.py
Regular package | A directory with __init__.py
Namespace package | A directory without __init__.py that Python can still import in Python 3.3+
Import root | A folder listed in sys.path where Python starts looking for packages and modules
site-packages | The installed package location inside the Python environment

Section: Template project layout

Use a generic project layout to understand the import behavior. The same checks apply locally, in Docker, and in production if the working directory is the project root.

Code (bash):
example-backend/
 main.py
 core/
 datasets/
 sentiment/
 router.py
 models/
 repositories/
 routers/
 schemas/
 services/
 email/
 verification.py
 oauth/
 google_service.py
 requirements.txt

If commands are run from example-backend, imports like from services.email.verification import parse_signup_verification_code and from datasets.sentiment.router import router resolve against local project folders.

title: Python Import Path and Package Checks
description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: Check the current import root

Run this from the project root. The blank first line in the output means the current working directory is on sys.path.

Code (bash):
cd example-backend
python -c "import sys; print('\n'.join(sys.path))"

Example output. The first blank line represents the current directory.

Code (bash):

/usr/lib/python312.zip
/usr/lib/python3.12
/usr/lib/python3.12/lib-dynload
/path/to/example-backend/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages

- If the current directory is the project root, local imports resolve from the project.
- If the command is run from the wrong directory, Python may not find the local package.
- If a top-level name conflicts with site-packages, Python may import the wrong package depending on the package type and path order.

title: Python Import Path and Package Checks
description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.
sectionTitle: Check where a package is imported from
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: Check where a package is imported from

Use __file__ and __path__ to inspect what Python imported. This confirms whether Python is using the local project folder or an installed package from site-packages.

Code (bash):
python -c "import datasets; print('datasets file:', getattr(datasets, '__file__', None)); print('datasets path:', list(getattr(datasets, '__path__', [])))"

python -c "import services; print('services file:', getattr(services, '__file__', None)); print('services path:', list(getattr(services, '__path__', [])))"

python -c "import routers; print('routers file:', getattr(routers, '__file__', None)); print('routers path:', list(getattr(routers, '__path__', [])))"

Interpret the result:

Table:
Output | Meaning
file points to /path/to/example-backend/services/__init__.py | services is a local regular package
file is None and path points to /path/to/example-backend/datasets | datasets is a local namespace package
file points to .venv/.../site-packages/datasets/__init__.py | Python imported the installed datasets library
ModuleNotFoundError | The import root is wrong or the package does not exist

title: Python Import Path and Package Checks
description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: Test exact imports

Test the actual imports used by the app. This verifies the module path without starting the server.

Code (bash):
python -c "import datasets.sentiment.router as r; print(r.__file__)"

python -c "from datasets.sentiment.router import router as topic_sentiment_router; print(topic_sentiment_router)"

python -c "from services.oauth.google_service import start_google_oauth; print(start_google_oauth)"

python -c "import main; print('main imported successfully')"

A good result for the router import should point to the local project file.

Code (bash):
/path/to/example-backend/datasets/sentiment/router.py

If Python finds the correct file but fails with cannot import name, the path is not the problem. The module was found, but the requested function, class, or variable does not exist under that exact name.

title: Python Import Path and Package Checks
description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: Regular package versus namespace package

A regular package is a directory with __init__.py. A namespace package is a directory without __init__.py that Python can still import in Python 3.3 and newer.

Table:
Package type | Directory shape | Behavior
Regular package | services/__init__.py | Python treats services as one explicit package folder
Namespace package | datasets/ without __init__.py | Python can assemble datasets from matching directories on sys.path

Namespace packages are valid Python. They are useful when a package is intentionally split across multiple locations. For normal app code, regular packages are more explicit.

Source type: Doc

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: When imports work without __init__.py

This works in Python 3.3+ when the project root is on sys.path and there is no conflicting top-level regular package that wins first.

Code (bash):
example-backend/
 main.py
 app1/
 services/
 email.py
 app2/
 services/
 payment.py

Code (python):
from app1.services.email import send_email
from app2.services.payment import charge

This is safer than importing from a generic top-level services package because app1 and app2 are the top-level names.

title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

sectionTitle: When __init__.py does not fix the problem
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: When __init__.py does not fix the problem

__init__.py does not fix a wrong import root. If Python starts searching from the wrong folder, it will not reach the correct package.

Code (bash):
project/
 example-backend/
 main.py
 services/
 __init__.py
 email/
 __init__.py
 verification.py

This command fails because Python searches project/services, but the real folder is project/example-backend/services.

Code (bash):
cd project
python -c "from services.email.verification import parse_signup_verification_code"

Fix it by running from the project root or setting PYTHONPATH.

Code (bash):
cd project/example-backend
python -c "from services.email.verification import parse_signup_verification_code"

cd project
PYTHONPATH=example-backend python -c "from services.email.verification import parse_signup_verification_code"

title: Python Import Path and Package Checks
description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: Top-level name conflicts

Generic top-level folder names can conflict with installed libraries. A common example is datasets, because datasets is also a third-party package name.

Table:
Local name | Conflict risk
datasets | High, because some projects install a package named datasets
core | Medium, generic name
models | Medium, generic name
schemas | Medium, generic name
services | Medium, generic name
routers | Lower, but still generic

If the app has a local top-level datasets package and also needs the external datasets library, the clean fix is to rename the local package or move it under a unique app package.

Code (bash):
example-backend/
 app/
 __init__.py
 datasets/
 __init__.py
 sentiment/
 __init__.py
 router.py

Code (python):
from app.datasets.sentiment.router import router as topic_sentiment_router
from datasets import load_dataset

title: Python Import Path and Package Checks
description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: What __init__.py changes

__init__.py changes package identity. It says this directory is a regular package and not just a namespace package portion.

Table:
With __init__.py | Without __init__.py
Regular package | Namespace package candidate
Has a concrete __file__ | __file__ may be None
Does not merge with same-named namespace portions | Can merge with same-named folders on sys.path
Can contain package initialization code | No package initialization file
Clearer for tools and humans | Valid, but more implicit

It does not solve wrong working directory, wrong PYTHONPATH, or bad top-level naming by itself.

title: Python Import Path and Package Checks
description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: When to add __init__.py

For normal backend code, use empty __init__.py files when you want explicit regular packages.

Code (bash):
touch core/__init__.py
touch datasets/__init__.py
touch datasets/sentiment/__init__.py
touch models/__init__.py
touch repositories/__init__.py
touch routers/__init__.py
touch schemas/__init__.py
touch services/__init__.py
touch services/email/__init__.py
touch services/oauth/__init__.py

- Add __init__.py to folders used in import paths.
- The files can be empty.
- Do not add __init__.py to documentation folders unless Python imports from them.
- If a top-level folder conflicts with a library name, prefer renaming or moving it under app instead of relying only on __init__.py.

title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Source type: Doc

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: __all__ in __init__.py

__all__ is not required for normal backend imports. It only controls wildcard imports such as from services import *.

Code (python):
__all__ = []

This means wildcard imports expose nothing by default. In most backend apps, empty __init__.py files are enough unless package-level exports are intentionally needed.

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: Check a missing imported name

If the module path resolves but Python says cannot import name, inspect the names exported by the module.

Code (bash):
python -c "import services.email.verification as v; print([name for name in dir(v) if 'verification' in name or 'signup' in name or 'code' in name])"

grep -n "def .*verification\|def .*signup\|def .*code" services/email/verification.py

This error means Python found the file but the requested function name does not exist in that module.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: Recommended long-term structure

The cleanest long-term backend structure is to use one unique top-level app package. This avoids generic top-level names such as datasets, services, models, and schemas.

Code (bash):
example-backend/
 main.py
 app/
 __init__.py

 __init__.py
 email/
 __init__.py
 verification.py
 oauth/
 __init__.py
 google_service.py

Code (python):
from app.datasets.sentiment.router import router as topic_sentiment_router
from app.services.email.verification import parse_signup_verification_code

This structure keeps external libraries available under their normal names.

Code (python):
from datasets import load_dataset

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: FastAPI and Docker checklist

- Run FastAPI from the project root, the folder that contains main.py.
- In Docker, set WORKDIR to the project root.
- Check sys.path from inside the container, not only from the host.
- Use import main as a quick runtime import test.
- Use module __file__ checks to confirm whether imports come from local code or site-packages.

Code (bash):
pwd
python -c "import sys; print('\n'.join(sys.path))"
python -c "import main; print('main imported successfully')"
python -c "import datasets.sentiment.router as r; print(r.__file__)"
python -c "import services; print(getattr(services, '__file__', None)); print(list(getattr(services, '__path__', [])))"

title: Python Import Path and Package Checks
description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Python Import Path and Package Checks

Category: Languages and Runtimes

Description: Debug Python import paths, regular packages, namespace packages, __init__.py behavior, and FastAPI backend import roots.

Intro: This guide explains how Python resolves imports in a backend project, how to inspect sys.path, how regular packages differ from namespace packages, when __init__.py matters, and how to test whether imports come from the local project or site-packages.

Section: Practical rule

- Imports depend on sys.path and the selected import root.
- __init__.py turns a directory into a regular package.
- No __init__.py means Python 3.3+ can still import the folder as a namespace package.
- Namespace packages are safe when the import root is controlled and top-level names do not conflict.
- Regular packages are clearer when the folder is part of the app codebase.
- Top-level name conflicts are best solved by using a unique app package, not by relying only on __init__.py.

title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2
description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Quick reference

Code (bash):
# check Git
git --version

# configure Git commit identity
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your_email@example.com"

# verify Git config
git config --global --list

# local machine key
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your_email@example.com" -f ~/.ssh/github_ed25519

# remove passphrase if needed
ssh-keygen -p -f ~/.ssh/github_ed25519

# print public key
cat ~/.ssh/github_ed25519.pub

# test GitHub SSH
ssh -T git@github.com

# check current repo remote
git remote -v

# convert HTTPS remote to SSH
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:USERNAME/REPO.git

# test Git operation
git pull

title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2
description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Purpose of this setup

GitHub no longer accepts normal account passwords for Git over HTTPS. If a repository uses an HTTPS remote, Git may ask for a username and token during pull, push, or clone.
SSH authentication avoids repeated username and token prompts. After the public key is added to GitHub and the repository remote uses the SSH URL, Git operations work through the SSH key.

title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2
description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Recommended key layout

Keep separate keys for separate purposes. Do not reuse the EC2 login key as a GitHub key, and do not copy the local GitHub private key to EC2.

Table:
Location | Key | Purpose
Local machine | ~/.ssh/github_ed25519 | Local machine to GitHub
Local machine | ~/.ssh/server-login.pem | Local machine to EC2
EC2 server | ~/.ssh/github_ec2_ed25519 | EC2 server to GitHub

title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2
description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Check existing SSH files

Before creating a new key, inspect the SSH directory. Existing .pem files are often server login keys and should not be uploaded to GitHub.

Code (bash):
ls -la ~/.ssh

- A .pem file is usually for logging in to a server.
- A GitHub SSH key usually has a private key and a matching .pub file.
- Only the .pub file is copied to GitHub.
- Never paste a private key into GitHub or any web form.

title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2
description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Configure Git identity

Set the Git author name and email on the machine before making commits. This controls the identity stored in commit metadata. It is separate from SSH authentication.

Code (bash):
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your_email@example.com"

Verify the global Git configuration.

Code (bash):
git config --global --list

- Use the same email as the GitHub account when possible.
- This does not authenticate GitHub access.
- SSH keys control pull, push, and clone access.
- Git identity controls the author information written into commits.

title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2
description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Create local GitHub SSH key

Create a dedicated GitHub key on the local machine. The -f value gives the key a clear filename instead of using the default id_ed25519.

Code (bash):
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your_email@example.com" -f ~/.ssh/github_ed25519

For a no-passphrase setup, press Enter when asked for the passphrase and press Enter again to confirm the empty passphrase.

title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

sectionTitle: Remove passphrase from an existing local key
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Remove passphrase from an existing local key

If the key already has a passphrase and Git asks for it on each operation, remove it with ssh-keygen -p. Enter the old passphrase first, then press Enter twice for an empty new passphrase.

Code (bash):
ssh-keygen -p -f ~/.ssh/github_ed25519

title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2
description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Configure SSH for GitHub locally

Add a GitHub host block so SSH always uses the intended key for github.com.

Code (bash):
nano ~/.ssh/config

Code (bash):
Host github.com
 HostName github.com
 User git
 IdentityFile ~/.ssh/github_ed25519
 IdentitiesOnly yes

Apply safe permissions.

Code (bash):
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/config
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/github_ed25519
chmod 644 ~/.ssh/github_ed25519.pub

sectionTitle: Add local public key to GitHub
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Add local public key to GitHub

Print the public key and copy the full output. It starts with ssh-ed25519 and ends with the comment used during key creation.

Code (bash):
cat ~/.ssh/github_ed25519.pub

- Open GitHub account settings.
- Go to SSH and GPG keys.
- Choose New SSH key.
- Use a clear title such as Local laptop GitHub key.
- Paste the public key.
- Save the key.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Test local GitHub SSH authentication

Test the connection before changing repository remotes. GitHub should confirm that authentication worked.

Code (bash):
ssh -T git@github.com

A successful response means the key works. GitHub does not provide shell access, so the message may also mention that shell access is not available.

sectionTitle: Convert an existing repository from HTTPS to SSH
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Convert an existing repository from HTTPS to SSH

SSH keys only help if the repository remote uses an SSH URL. If the remote still uses HTTPS, Git can continue asking for username and token.

Code (bash):
git remote -v

If the output uses HTTPS, change origin to the SSH form.

Code (bash):
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:USERNAME/REPO.git
git remote -v

Test with a normal Git operation.

Code (bash):
git pull

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Create EC2 GitHub SSH key

For EC2, create a separate key on the EC2 server. Do not copy the local private key to the server.

Code (bash):
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "ec2-github-access" -f ~/.ssh/github_ec2_ed25519

For a production pull workflow, an empty passphrase is practical because deploy and pull commands may need to run without interactive input.

sectionTitle: Add EC2 public key to GitHub
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Add EC2 public key to GitHub

Print the EC2 public key and add it to GitHub. Prefer a repository deploy key when the server only needs access to one repository.

Code (bash):
cat ~/.ssh/github_ec2_ed25519.pub

Table:
GitHub location | Use case | Access scope
Repository Settings > Deploy keys | Best for production pull access to one repo | Single repository
Account Settings > SSH and GPG keys | Simpler when deploy keys are unavailable | All repos the account can access

- For a production server, prefer a deploy key if available.
- Leave write access disabled when EC2 only needs to pull code.
- Use account SSH keys only when deploy keys are unavailable or the server needs access to multiple repositories.

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Daily verification commands

Use these commands when Git unexpectedly asks for a username, token, or passphrase.

Code (bash):
# check Git identity
git config --global --list

# check remote URL
git remote -v

# test GitHub SSH auth
ssh -T git@github.com

# check which key SSH tries
ssh -vT git@github.com

# check SSH config file
cat ~/.ssh/config

# check key files
ls -la ~/.ssh

Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Expected final state

- Git global identity is configured on the machine.
- Local Git operations use git@github.com:USERNAME/REPO.git.
- EC2 Git operations use git@github.com:USERNAME/REPO.git.
- Git no longer asks for GitHub username and token.
- No passphrase prompt appears if the key was created or updated with an empty passphrase.
- Local and EC2 use different private keys.

title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2
description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: GitHub SSH Authentication for Local and EC2

Category: Git

Description: Set up GitHub SSH authentication without repeated username and token prompts, using separate keys for local development and EC2.

Intro: This workflow replaces HTTPS username and token prompts with SSH authentication. It covers Git identity, local machine setup, EC2 setup, GitHub key placement, SSH remote URLs, and daily verification commands.

Section: Common mistakes

- Skipping Git identity setup before making commits.
- Confusing Git identity with GitHub authentication.
- Adding the private key instead of the .pub key to GitHub.
- Keeping the repository remote as HTTPS after setting up SSH.
- Using the EC2 login .pem key as the GitHub key.
- Copying the local private key to EC2.
- Adding the EC2 key to account settings when a repo-specific deploy key would be safer.
- Expecting Deploy keys under account settings. Deploy keys are under repository settings.

title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker
description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Intro: This workflow runs the Bizdocs e-signature service inside a Debian Docker container while keeping the Arch host clean. The host provides the smart card reader through pcscd and the container runs Bizdocs, GUI forwarding, Java, and signing tools. The normal browser on the host can sign documents through the Bizdocs local service.

Section: Overview

- Use the Arch host only for Docker, X11 access, and pcscd.
- Use the Debian container for Bizdocs, GUI libraries, Java, and signing tools.
- Share the smart card reader with the container through /run/pcscd/pcscd.comm.
- Share documents and installers through ./work mounted as /work.
- Run Bizdocs inside the container and keep the host browser outside the container.
- Use network_mode: host so the host browser can reach the Bizdocs service on 127.0.0.1:2151.

title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker
description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Intro: This workflow runs the Bizdocs e-signature service inside a Debian Docker container while keeping the Arch host clean. The host provides the smart card reader through pcscd and the container runs Bizdocs, GUI forwarding, Java, and signing tools. The normal browser on the host can sign documents through the Bizdocs local service.

Section: Working result

The completed setup uses this chain. This confirms that signing can work from the normal host browser while Bizdocs runs inside the container.

Code (bash):
Arch browser
-> http://127.0.0.1:2151
-> Bizdocs service inside Debian container
-> /run/pcscd/pcscd.comm from Arch host
-> ACS ACR39T / ACR39U reader
-> e-signature card
-> signed document

Source type: Doc

Title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Intro: This workflow runs the Bizdocs e-signature service inside a Debian Docker container while keeping the Arch host clean. The host provides the smart card reader through pcscd and the container runs Bizdocs, GUI forwarding, Java, and signing tools. The normal browser on the host can sign documents through the Bizdocs local service.

Section: Install host packages

Install the low-level smart card stack on the Arch host. This keeps the reader support on the host and avoids installing the signing application directly on the host.

Code (bash):
sudo pacman -S pcsclite ccid pcsc-tools xorg-xhost
sudo systemctl enable --now pcscd.service

sectionTitle: Test the smart card reader on the host
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Intro: This workflow runs the Bizdocs e-signature service inside a Debian Docker container while keeping the Arch host clean. The host provides the smart card reader through pcscd and the container runs Bizdocs, GUI forwarding, Java, and signing tools. The normal browser on the host can sign documents through the Bizdocs local service.

Section: Test the smart card reader on the host

Plug in the ACS reader and insert the card. pcsc_scan should detect the reader, card state, and ATR. Stop the command with Ctrl+C after confirming the card is visible.

Code (bash):
pcsc_scan

A successful result shows the ACS reader and Card inserted. The reader may appear as ACR39T or ACR39U depending on the device firmware and driver reporting.

Title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Intro: This workflow runs the Bizdocs e-signature service inside a Debian Docker container while keeping the Arch host clean. The host provides the smart card reader through pcscd and the container runs Bizdocs, GUI forwarding, Java, and signing tools. The normal browser on the host can sign documents through the Bizdocs local service.

Section: Create the workspace

Create one movable workspace for the compose file, installers, test PDFs, and signed outputs. The work folder is mounted into the container as /work.

Code (bash):
mkdir -p ~/projects/esign/work
cd ~/projects/esign

Place the Bizdocs Debian package in the work folder.

Code (bash):
ls work
# bizdocs-eimza_1.2.6_amd64.deb

title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker
description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Intro: This workflow runs the Bizdocs e-signature service inside a Debian Docker container while keeping the Arch host clean. The host provides the smart card reader through pcscd and the container runs Bizdocs, GUI forwarding, Java, and signing tools. The normal browser on the host can sign documents through the Bizdocs local service.

Section: Create compose.yaml

Use Debian as the container base. Host networking is used because the host browser must reach the Bizdocs local service. X11 is mounted so the Bizdocs GUI can open from the container.

Code (bash):
nano compose.yaml

Code (bash):
services:
 esign:
 image: debian:12
 container_name: esign
 network_mode: host
 stdin_open: true
 tty: true
 environment:
 - DISPLAY=${DISPLAY}
 - LIBGL_ALWAYS_SOFTWARE=1
 volumes:
 - /run/pcscd/pcscd.comm:/run/pcscd/pcscd.comm
 - /tmp/.X11-unix:/tmp/.X11-unix
 - ./work:/work
 working_dir: /work

Title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Intro: This workflow runs the Bizdocs e-signature service inside a Debian Docker container while keeping the Arch host clean. The host provides the smart card reader through pcscd and the container runs Bizdocs, GUI forwarding, Java, and signing tools. The normal browser on the host can sign documents through the Bizdocs local service.

Section: Start the container

Start the container and enter it. The prompt changes to root inside the container. The /work folder inside the container maps to ./work on the host.

Code (bash):
cd ~/projects/esign
docker compose up -d
docker exec -it esign bash

Code (bash):
pwd
ls
cat /etc/os-release

Install tools for smart card testing, GUI forwarding, TLS certificates, and basic runtime support. iproute2 provides ss for checking the local Bizdocs service port.

 libxcursor1 \
 libxrandr2 \
 libxinerama1 \
 libxi6 \
 libxxf86vm1 \
 libxrender1 \
 libxext6 \
 libxfixes3 \
 libxkbcommon0 \
 libxkbcommon-x11-0 \
 libgtk-3-0 \
 libnss3 \
 libasound2 \
 x11-apps

update-ca-certificates

The host provides the smart card reader through pcscd and the container runs Bizdocs, GUI forwarding, Java, and signing tools. Section: Enable host GUI access

Run xhost on the Arch host, not inside the container. This allows the container to open GUI windows through the host X11 session.

Title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Intro: This workflow runs the Bizdocs e-signature service inside a Debian Docker container while keeping the Arch host clean. The host provides the smart card reader through pcscd and the container runs Bizdocs, GUI forwarding, Java, and signing tools. The normal browser on the host can sign documents through the Bizdocs local service.

Section: Install Bizdocs

Install the Bizdocs Debian package from the shared /work folder. The package installs the launcher under /opt/bizdocs-eimza.

Code (bash):
cd /work
apt install -y ./bizdocs-eimza_1.2.6_amd64.deb

If dependencies are missing, fix broken dependencies and run the install command again.

Code (bash):
apt --fix-broken install -y
apt install -y ./bizdocs-eimza_1.2.6_amd64.deb

Confirm the launcher exists.

Code (bash):
ls -la /opt/bizdocs-eimza
file /opt/bizdocs-eimza/BIZdocs-eLauncher

The normal browser on the host can sign documents through the Bizdocs local service.

Section: Start Bizdocs

Run the Bizdocs launcher from inside the container. Wait until the GUI says the signature service is ready.

Code (bash):
/opt/bizdocs-eimza/BIZdocs-eLauncher

- Expected GUI state: İmza servise hazır.
- Bizdocs may update itself on first launch.
- Keep the Bizdocs window open while signing from the browser.

title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker
description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.
text:
Source type: Doc

Title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Intro: This workflow runs the Bizdocs e-signature service inside a Debian Docker container while keeping the Arch host clean. The host provides the smart card reader through pcscd and the container runs Bizdocs, GUI forwarding, Java, and signing tools. The normal browser on the host can sign documents through the Bizdocs local service.

Section: Check the local signing service

With Bizdocs running, check that BIZdocs.Core listens on the local service port.

Code (bash):
ss -lntp

Expected service:

Code (bash):
127.0.0.1:2151 users:(("BIZdocs.Core",pid=...,fd=...))

From the Arch host, test the same port. A 404 response is acceptable because the root path is not the signing endpoint. The important part is that the connection is not refused.

Code (bash):
curl http://127.0.0.1:2151
# 404 page not found

title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker
description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Intro: This workflow runs the Bizdocs e-signature service inside a Debian Docker container while keeping the Arch host clean. The host provides the smart card reader through pcscd and the container runs Bizdocs, GUI forwarding, Java, and signing tools. The normal browser on the host can sign documents through the Bizdocs local service.

Section: Sign from the host browser

Use the normal browser on the Arch host. The browser talks to the Bizdocs local service through 127.0.0.1:2151 while Bizdocs runs inside the container.

- Start the container.
- Start Bizdocs inside the container.
- Wait until Bizdocs shows İmza servise hazır.
- Open the signing page in the host browser.
- Start the signing operation.
- Choose the certificate and enter the PIN if requested.
- Confirm that the signed document is completed.

title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker
description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Source type: Doc

Title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker

Category: Linux Desktop Workflow

Description: Run Bizdocs e-signature service in a Debian container on an Arch host, with ACS smart card reader access through pcscd and browser signing through localhost.

Intro: This workflow runs the Bizdocs e-signature service inside a Debian Docker container while keeping the Arch host clean. The host provides the smart card reader through pcscd and the container runs Bizdocs, GUI forwarding, Java, and signing tools. The normal browser on the host can sign documents through the Bizdocs local service.

Section: TLS certificate fix

If Bizdocs fails while downloading the signing package and shows x509: certificate signed by unknown authority, update the container certificate store and restart Bizdocs.

Code (bash):
apt install -y ca-certificates curl
update-ca-certificates

curl -I https://example-signing-domain.com

If curl returns HTTP 200 or another valid HTTP response, restart Bizdocs and retry signing.

Code (bash):
pkill -f BIZdocs || true
pkill -f BIZdocs.Core || true
pkill -f BIZdocs-eLauncher || true

/opt/bizdocs-eimza/BIZdocs-eLauncher

Title: Bizdocs e-Signature in Docker
